description: Focuses on the art of the Mediterranean area and Europe between ca. A.D. 350 and 1600. By means of movements and artists, examines the cultural identities of the Christian, Islamic, and Early Modern worlds.

title: Early Modern and Modern Art in Europe and America
description: The visual arts from the 17th to the 21st centuries. Explores the purposes and meaning of painting, sculpture, and architecture in relation to artists, viewers, and historical events.

title: History of Asian Art: Arts of China
description: An introduction to the arts and visual culture of China from Neolithic to modern times. Themes includes the representation of power, death and the afterlife, popular culture and elite arts, landscape and women's painting, and concludes with contemporary China.

title: History of Asian Art: Arts of Japan
description: Examines compelling images and objects of spirit and power created in Japan over many centuries. Themes include Buddhist icons, narrative illustration, popular prints, architecture, manga, and the avant-garde. Japanese interactions with Korean, Chinese, and European culture are emphasized.

title: History of Asian Art: Arts of Islam
description: Examines past and present Islamic art, spanning 1,500 years and extending from the Americas through Indonesia. Themes include Islam as a globalizing force, the definition of "Islamic," and the competing roles of religion and politics in making art.

title: Arts of Korea
description: Introduction to the arts and visual culture of Korea from the Neolithic period to the Joseon Dynasty. Themes include the conceptions of life, death, and afterlife, expressions of power and privilege, leisure, knowledge, and trade.

title: Image Collision: A Multicultural Approach to Images and Their Users
description: Comparisons between present-day visual culture and pre-modern art to re-evaluate the ways in which one understands the contemporary histories of underrepresented groups in the United States.

title: Introduction to Ancient Egyptian Archaeology and Art
description: Introduction to the art and archaeology of ancient Egypt from prehistory through the Late Period. Themes covered include kingship and monumentality, the materialization of religious beliefs and practices, and non-royal and non-elite cultural expression.

title: Disneyland
description: From Dole Whips to roller coasters, the focus is on the innovations in industrial automation and manufacturing that made Disneyland’s attractions possible. Discusses fandom, guest experiences, operating procedures, technical and design patents, and how rides work.

title: Studies in Ancient Art
description: Topics in Egyptian, Prehistoric, and Etruscan art of the Mediterranean area treated with specific reference to relevant cultural and historical settings.  Specialized courses in Greek and Roman art are also taught.

title: Topics in Ancient Egyptian Art and Archaeology
description: Varying topics in ancient Egyptian art and archaeology, from the Predynastic Period through the Late Period. Developments in art and archaeology are analyzed through their sociopolitical and historical contexts.

title: Studies in Greek Art
description: Topics in Greek art, architecture, and topography from the Prehistoric period through the end of the fourth century B.C.E.

title: Studies in Roman Art
description: Topics in Hellenistic and Roman art and architecture; stresses historical and political background.

title: Studies in Medieval Art
description: Specialized topics in Medieval art and architecture in Europe, the Mediterranean area, and the Near East between the fourth and fifteenth centuries.  Examples:  the Art of the Migration Period, Medieval City.

title: Studies in Western Medieval Art
description: Selected topics on the development of art and architecture in Western Europe between ca. 700 and 1400. Examples: Romanesque painting, Gothic architecture.

title: Studies in Renaissance and Baroque Art
description: Selected topics on the art and architecture of Europe between the fifteenth and eighteenth centuries. Examples: Renaissance and Baroque prints, Bruegel to Rubens.

title: Studies in Southern Renaissance Art
description: Selected topics determined by individual faculty members exploring historical developments and individual artists of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries in Italy and Spain. Examples: Renaissance Venice, Age of Michelangelo.

title: Studies in Southern Baroque Art
description: Selected topics determined by individual faculty members exploring historical developments and individual artists of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries in Italy and Spain. Example: Rome in the seventeenth century.

title: Studies in Northern Baroque Art
description: Selected topics determined by individual faculty members exploring historical developments and individual artists of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries in Northern Europe. Example: the Age of Rembrandt.

title: Modern European Art: From Impressionism to the Fauves
description: History of European painting and urban transformation from 1851 to 1907, when Paris stood strong as the unquestioned cultural capital of the nineteenth century.

title: Modern European Art: From Cubism to Surrealism
description: History of European painting, sculpture, and design from 1907 to 1940, when Paris and painting lost their dominance as other cultural centers and other media claimed renewed importance.

title: Topics in Modern European Art
description: Selected topics within the period 1643 to 1940.

title: History of Contemporary Art
description: Selected topics exploring historical developments of contemporary art from 1945 to the present. Examples: American Art 1945-1989, European Art 1945-1989, Art After 1989. Works of art are studied as cultural, social, and political practices.

title: Topics in Contemporary Art
description: Selected topics exploring artistic movements, artists, and/or issues in contemporary art from 1945 to the present. Examples include Junk Art, Countercultures, Art and Politics Now. Works of art are studied as cultural, social, and political practices.

title: Studies in Modern Architecture
description: Architecture and related design practices from the late eighteenth century through 1945 are studied in relation to social, aesthetic, technological, and political questions.

title: Studies in Architecture after 1945
description: Architecture and related design practices from 1945 through present are studied in relation to social, aesthetic, technological, and political questions.

title: Topics in the History of Modern and Contemporary Architecture
description: Varying topics from the late eighteenth century to the present. Architecture and related design practices are studied in relation to social, aesthetic, technological, and political questions.

title: Studies in Asian Art
description: Topics include visual studies in China, Japan, Korea and India.

title: Later Imperial China
description: Investigates the uses, subjects, styles, and social contexts of art made in the later Imperial dynasties, whether court art, religious art, women's painting, scholar painting, or art for the market.

title: Modern China
description: Examines the evolution and media of Chinese art and visual culture in the context of modern China's sweeping historical, social, and political changes.

title: Ancient India
description: Examines the visual and religious history of the region defined as "India" today, but necessarily encompassing modern Bangladesh and Pakistan.  Culminates with the supposed Golden Age of the Gupta empire and its far-reaching legacies.

title: Medieval India
description: Begins with the Gupta period's aesthetic legacies in South Asia's architecture, sculpture, and painting. Explores the dispersal of Islam throughout South Asia, including the Muslim communities of southern India.

title: Topics in the Art and Architecture of India
description: Studies in selected areas of Art and Architecture of India. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Contemporary Japan
description: Study of various media of Japanese art from the time of war defeat in 1945 until the present. Topics include artists' responses to the nuclear bomb, the aesthetics of the "economic miracle," avant-garde groups, manga, and innovations in architecture.

title: Asian American Art, Contemporary
description: Study of the Asian American experience in contemporary art and visual culture. Art by Asian Americans of diverse backgrounds as well as the history of visualization of Asian identities in American art/visual culture.

title: Modern African American Art
description: Investigates the history of modern African American art; emphasis on the politics of representation. Examines art in a variety of media from material culture and textiles to painting and photography. Issues of migration, nationalism, gender, sexuality, and hybridity are discussed.

title: Contemporary African American Art
description: Investigates the history of contemporary African American art; emphasis on the politics of representation. Explores art in a variety of media: painting, sculpture, photography, installation, and new media. Cultural politics, appropriation, identity, gender, sexuality, hybridity and civil rights issues discussed.

title: African American Women in Art
description: Examines depictions of and by African American women in art and popular culture through a variety of media including textiles, painting, sculpture, photography, and installation. Focuses on African American women's experiences, perspectives, and strategies for contemporary representation.

title: African Americans and Photography
description: Explores depictions of and by African Americans through photography. Examines the history of photography in relationship to African American culture through a variety of media from early daguerreotype processes to digital imagery.

title: Early American Art
description: An examination of American visual culture from 1620 to 1860 from the era of European exploration and colonization of the New World to the beginning of the Civil War.

title: Nineteenth Century American Art
description: An exploration of American visual culture from 1860 to 1900, including paintings, sculpture, and photographs produced during the Civil War and during the decades of prosperity that followed.

title: Modern American Art
description: Focusing on the period from 1900 until 1965, this course considers the emergence and institutionalization of modern art in the United States.

title: Topics in American Art
description: Varying topics within the period 1620 to 1970. Works of art are studied in their cultural, social, and political contexts.

title: Topics in the History of Modern Latin American and Latinx Art
description: Varying topics exploring the history of modernism in Latin American and Latinx visual art. Art movements and artworks are analyzed through their socio-political and historical contexts with a special attention to race, ethnicity, gender, and migration.

title: Topics in Museum Studies
description: Addresses the historical and contemporary function of the museum as an instructional device.  The function of exhibitions in the public sphere, and the roles of curators, educators, and the public are analyzed.

title: Topics in Visual Studies
description: Interdisciplinary topics on the cultural analysis of visual artifacts and practices.

title: Art History Methods
description: Theory and practice of art history, with an emphasis on formal and social models of analyzing and writing about art.

title: Ethics, Law, and Art
description: Addresses a broad range of ethical and legal issues pertaining to art, ownership, looting and theft, museum policies, and cultural heritage.

title: Art History Social Hour
description: Students meet members of the faculty and Art History Undergraduate Association, and explore the exceptional opportunities available for internships, independent research, and field trips through the Art History Department’s Steckler Family endowment.

title: Advanced Seminar: Topics in Art History
description: Discussion and report-oriented seminar with emphasis on reading, writing, and thinking about problems in art history. Examples: Gothic Cathedral, Portraiture East and West.

title: Independent Study in Art History
description: Supervised, but independent reading or research on art historical topics.

title: Ancient India
description: Examines the visual and religious history of the region defined as "India" today, but necessarily encompassing modern Bangladesh and Pakistan. Culminates with the supposed Golden Age of the Gupta empire and its far-reaching legacies.

title: Medieval India
description: Begins with the Gupta period's aesthetic legacies in South Asia's architecture, sculpture, and painting. Explores the dispersal of Islam throughout South Asia, including the Muslim communities of southern India.

title: Ethics, Law, and Art
description: Addresses a broad range of ethical and legal issues pertaining to art, ownership, looting and theft, museum policies, and cultural heritage.

title: Master's Seminar: Topics in Art History
description: Studies in selected areas of Art History. Examples include Gothic Cathedral, Portraiture East and West.

title: Master's Thesis Research
description: Research and writing of the Master's thesis.

title: University Teaching
description: Limited to teaching assistants.

title: ArtsCore
description: An introduction to the arts in general, and to the arts at UCI. Concentration on (1) the interdisciplinary nature of the arts, and (2) the content of particular arts disciplines.

title: Digital Media: Exhibition
description: A discussion and critique course in all media arts. Includes a review of key media arts concepts and the preparation of an end-of-quarter exhibition.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual study or directed projects as arranged with faculty member.

title: Asian American Histories
description: Examines and compares diverse experiences of major Asian American groups since the mid-nineteenth century. Topics include origins of emigration; the formation and transformation of community; gender and family life; changing roles of Asian Americans in American society. Formerly ASIANAM 60A.

title: The U.S. and Asia
description: Explores the historical and contemporary transnational linkages between the U.S. and regions in Asia and their resultant flows of people, goods, and ideas. Attention given to the role of militarism and processes of globalization, and the histories of cultural contact/conflict.

title: Asian American Communities
description: Examines the renewal of Asian immigration following World War II. Explores contemporary Asian American populations and communities in the U.S., and the impact of contemporary Asian immigration on the U.S. political economy and social order.

title: Asian Americans and Race
description: Analyzes the Asian American experience in comparative perspective, which includes comparisons of different ethnic and racial groups, and across gender and class. Possible topics include labor, economy, politics, migration, nation, popular culture, gender, family, sexuality, and multiraciality.

title: Asian American Stories
description: Examines stories from Asian American communities through literary texts and other media. Selected themes may include the following: dislocation/relocation, finding/inventing a usable past, poetics/politics in language, identities/ethnicities.

title: Asian Americans and the Media
description: Investigates popular representations of and cultural productions by Asian Pacific Americans and Asians in the Americas from the late-19th century to the present. Cultural media may include political cartoons, film/television, popular music, visual art, blogs/Web sites, and performance.

title: Research Methodologies for Asian American Studies
description: Explores various research methodologies for Asian American Studies combining theoretical knowledge with field research. Goals: conduct field research about immigrants and refugees from Asia. Topics vary: migration and labor, assimilation and cultural preservation, cultural expressions in the diaspora.

title: Asian American Writers
description: Literary analysis of Asian American writers' representations of issues of identity, class, history among others. Variety of literary forms—novel, poem, drama, essay—included in a study of a variety of Asian American ethnic groups.

title: Asian American History
description: Introduction to important themes in the history of people of Asian ancestry in the United States from the nineteenth century to the present.

title: Asian American Art History
description: Investigation of Asian American experience expressed by art and visual culture throughout the twentieth century. Art by Asian Americans of diverse backgrounds as well as the history of cultural visualization of Asian identities in American art/visual culture.

title: Topics in Asian American Film and Video
description: Topics include histories of Asian American film and video, including documentaries, experimental, short subjects, feature-length independent film, and other forms of cinematic expression. Explores issues of identity (national, racial, gendered, among others).

title: Asian Americans and Popular Culture
description: Focuses on Asian Americans' relationship to popular culture as both producers and consumers. Topics include consumer cultures and subcultures, cyberspace and public space, popular music, indy comics and other print media.

title: Undocumented Immigrant Experiences
description: Examines the experiences of undocumented immigrants and the policies that structure their educational, economic, social, and political participation.

title: Comparative Minority Politics
description: Examines the political experiences of Blacks, Latinos, and Asian Americans in the United States from roughly 1950 to the present. Focuses on how each group has pursued political empowerment via both conventional political channels and social movements.

title: Asian American Labor
description: Explores history of Asian Americans and work from the nineteenth century to the present. Areas of study include migration, colonialism, family, social organization, and work culture.

title: Race and Urban Space
description: Examines how ethnic and racial processes shape and structure interactions in urban settings, such as schools, housing, employment, and public spaces, with attention to the international impact of globalization and postcolonial forces.

title: Arabs and Muslims in the US
description: Offers a critical academic study of Islam in America, focusing on the core tenets of belief, the diversity of practices, and historical transformations that have taken place since the arrival of Muslims in the United States.

title: The Politics of Protest
description: Examines the Civil Rights, Black Power, and women’s movements in relationship to the Asian American movement. Uses social movement theories to illuminate the cases, and the cases to critique and revise the theories.

title: Special Topics in Asian American Studies
description: Analyzes a variety of themes in Asian American Studies—identity, history, culture—from various interdisciplinary perspectives in humanities, arts, social sciences.

title: Korean American Studies
description: Explores the factors that have distinctly shaped the Korean American experience, including patterns of racial domination, the profile of immigrant flow, immigrant roles in the urban political economy, politics in Korea, and the role of the church.

title: Vietnamese American Studies
description: Studies the resettlement of Vietnamese in the United States following their exodus from Southeast Asia. Topics include the Vietnam War, the 1975 evacuation, boat and land refugees, the shaping of Vietnamese communities, and Vietnamese American literature.

title: Japanese American Studies
description: Studies the settlement of Japanese in Hawaii and the continental United States since the late 19th century. Topics include sugar plantations, development of rural Japanese America, World War II internment, post-War community development, and persistence of Japanese American identity.

title: South Asian American Studies
description: Examines and compares the experiences of South Asian immigrants in the U.S. over time. Looks at the economic, political, and social positions of the immigrants, with special emphasis on religious changes and the changes in the second and later generations.

title: Southeast Asian American Studies
description: Analyzes experiences of refugees and immigrants from Southeast Asia, which may include those from Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, and the Philippines. Examines political and economic factors for their exodus and how they reconstruct their identities, histories, and communities.

title: Filipina/Filipino American Studies
description: Explores the experience of Filipina/Filipino Americans from the era of Spanish colonization of the Philippines to present-day community formations in the United States, with special emphasis on the 20th century. Topics include colonialism, nation, migration, gender, and culture.

title: Asian American Women
description: Examines the representations and experiences of Asian American women from diverse perspectives. Explores the commonalities and differences among various groups of Asian American women, with particular focus on history, culture, values, and family roles.

title: Topics in Intersectionality
description: Topics include intersectional analysis of various themes related to ethnicity, gender, sexuality, and race within Asian American communities.

title: Race and Citizenship
description: Explores historical and contemporary patterns of racialization in relation to citizenship. Topics may include racial categorization, immigration, and comparative racialization.

title: Politics of Animal Rights
description: Examines animal rights/welfare movement’s efforts to transform moral, practical, and legal standing of nonhuman animals in contemporary U.S. Explores intersection of racism, sexism, and speciesism informed by theories of race and ethnicity, including Asian American Studies.

title: Independent Study
description: Directed reading and research in consultation with a faculty member. Substantial written work required.

title: Theory and Methods in Asian American Studies
description: Introduction to the intersection of the social sciences, humanities, and other fields that constitute the theory and methodology of Asian American Studies. Focuses on the interventions and contestations within Asian American Studies that have transformed the discipline in recent years.

title: Contemporary Issues in Asian American Studies
description: Examines the interrelations between history, theory, and race in the aftermath of the twentieth-century decolonial movements, offering an account of race through postcolonial and postnationalist approaches in comparative contexts. Considers the interventions made by transnational feminist and racialized queer critiques.

title: Leadership and Social Change in Asian American Communities
description: Introduces students to models of community-engaged learning and leadership that are central to the field of Asian American studies. Designed to expose students to histories and models of organizing in Asian American communities and forms of community-based research.

title: Introduction to Asian American Studies Research
description: Introduces students to research topics and methods within the field of Asian American studies. Aims to expose students to core and affiliated faculty to help them identify possible advisors for master's research projects or members of doctoral committees.

title: Graduate Topics in Asian American Studies
description: Seminars on various topics in Asian American Studies.

title: Advanced Topics in Asian American Studies
description: Seminar covering various areas of research within Asian American Studies as an interdisciplinary field. Recommended for advanced graduate students.

title: Directed Research
description: Directed graduate study/research in Asian American Studies.

title: Directed Reading
description: Readings focused on specialized topics in consultation with, and with the consent of, a faculty member.

title: University Teaching
description: Limited to teaching assistants.

title: Foundations of Business Analytics
description: Provides overview of the field of business analytics, and the theory and practice underpinnings of the three MSBA curricular tracks: Data Analytics, Marketing Analytics, and Operations Analytics. Provides students with a working knowledge of the R programming language.

title: Statistics for Data Science
description: Methods of statistical inference, emphasizing applications to administrative and management decision problems. Topics include classical estimation and hypothesis testing, regression, correlation, analysis of variance, decision analysis, and forecasting.

title: Management Science for Analytics
description: An introduction to computer-based models for decision making. Topics include optimization (linear programming, integer programming, network flow models) and computer simulation. Uses spreadsheets extensively, including Excel built-in and add-in packages.

title: Foundations of Marketing
description: Introduction to the field of marketing. Objectives include developing familiarity with fundamental concepts, theories, and techniques in marketing, and acquainting students with the type of decisions made by marketing managers, including customer targeting, product, pricing, distribution, promotion, and research.

title: Operations Analytics
description: Introduction to strategic and tactical issues in production and operations management. A blend of quantitative and qualitative considerations. Topics include product planning, process design, capacity management, production planning, inventory control, distribution management, just-in-time manufacturing, quality management.

title: MSBA ProSeminar
description: Provides students in the Merage School Master of Science in Business Analytics program with information and practical skills for success in the MSBA program and for business career planning.

title: Data and Programming for Analytics
description: Challenges and teaches students how to handle data, from initial data acquisition to final data analysis. Provides students with a working knowledge of the Python programming language.

title: Business Analytics Internship
description: Students apply analytics and big data knowledge obtained from classroom theory through work experiences. Provides knowledge of career opportunities, an understanding of the role analytics plays in business environments, and preparation for the business analytics profession.

title: Marketing Analytics
description: Introduces students to quantitative marketing analytics methods and teaches them how to apply the methods to business problems of interest. Topics include: resource allocation and ROI, marketing mix analytics, customer analytics, market segmentation and targeting, advertising, promotions, and digital marketing.

title: Machine Learning for Analytics
description: Introduces methods to mine data repositories for business intelligence to facilitate analytical decision-making. Topics include clustering for market segmentation, association rules to discover relationships between different purchase decisions, and  Naive-Bayes classification techniques for decision making using decision-trees.

title: Deep Learning and Applications
description: Provides students with an understanding of specific topics in machine learning including networks and deep learning, improving neural networks, structuring deep learning projects, and convolutional neural networks.

title: Natural Language Processing and Applications
description: Focuses on providing students knowledge of advanced techniques for data analytics. Students deepen their understanding of topics including dimension reduction, text mining and topic modeling, sentiment analysis, collaborative filtering, and causal estimation.

title: Customer and Social Analytics
description: Examines how business managers can leverage Web 2.0 and social media analytics to create value for their organizations and customers. Topics include digital and social strategies; Web and search analytics; display and mobile advertising; social networks and influence; social media.

title: Business Data Management
description: Provides essential knowledge for business managers to store and utilize data effectively. Aims to introduce the core concepts in databases, data warehouses, and big data on cloud. Offers an in-depth understanding of each technology’s core concepts with hands-on programming.

title: Supply Chain Analytics
description: Introduces students to the tools and strategies to effectively match supply and demand. Focuses on the coordination of material and information flows in supply chains. Recent innovations are also discussed, including globalization, the impact of electronic commerce, and sustainability issues.

title: The Art and Science of Applied Forecast Modeling
description: Covers core and advanced techniques of forecasting. Emphasizes the innovation required to develop accurate models, inspires the need to break rules, leverages ideation needed to discover data sources and engineer variables, and seeks ways to build new models.

title: Predictive Analytics
description: Deals with predicting entities (forecasting) and predicting membership of known groups (classification). Blends methodologies of forecasting and data mining, focusing on multiple regression, logistic regression, neural nets, ARIMA, discriminate analysis, and k-nearest neighbors, and applying these methods to managerial problems/decision-making.

title: Special Topics in Business Analytics
description: Studies in selected areas of business analytics. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Big Data Management Systems
description: Provides an introductory-level overview to the emerging technologies in database systems. Students gain critical insight into big data challenges, the choices of different systems and technologies available, relative advantages/disadvantages, and unique aspects of different systems.

title: Business Analytics Capstone Prep
description: Prep course for the required capstone project. Students apply the knowledge and methods acquired through the program’s coursework to an applied business analytics problem. Requirements include project selection, submission of project plan, and data collection/analysis.

title: Business Analytics Capstone Project
description: Continuation of the capstone project. Students apply the knowledge and methods acquired through the coursework in the program to an applied business analytics problem. Work includes data analysis, completed research paper, and final presentation.

title: Individual Study
description: Individual study under the direction of a selected faculty member.

title: Freshman Seminar
description: Weekly meetings consisting of presentations by faculty, professional staff, and Peer Academic Advisors provide information about the School of Biological Sciences, campus resources, learning skills, and special programs/opportunities.

title: Freshman Seminar
description: Faculty presentations and readings focused on the structure, function, opportunities, and current issues in the biological sciences.

title: Solutions in Science
description: Students will be introduced to approaches that can be used to solve scientific problems. These methods can be utilized in introductory to advanced classes and will allow students to become independent thinkers.

title: EASE Seminar
description: Seminars designed to help students achieve success in STEM courses. Provides collaborative learning environment facilitated by undergraduate mentor. Students develop critical thinking and study skills. Also receive guidance to campus resources that assist acclimation to University.

title: Topics and Careers in Ecology and Evolution
description: Introduces students to topics, research opportunities, and career options in ecology and evolutionary biology.

title: Topics in Academic Success
description: There is an increasing awareness of the importance of non-academic factors (like belongingness) for student success. Provides students with peer mentoring and tools specific to different career stages that have been shown to improve student outcomes.

title: Career Decision-Making
description: An introductory course designed to facilitate the career decision-making process. Decision-making processes, values, and standardized tests of aptitudes, interests, and values are utilized with non-test data in appraising biological sciences career options.

title: Non-Health Sciences Career Exploration
description: A survey course designed to assist students in exploring non-health science career options. Lectures by professionals in various fields.

title: Introduction to Field Biology
description: Field excursions to introduce students to local field sites and resources.

title: Tropical Biology: Race to Save the Tropics
description: Population growth combines with tropical resource consumption by industrialized nations to cause high rates of deforestation, pollution, habitat fragmentation, and extinction of species. Discusses tropical biomes, their population, community,  ecosystem processes, and possible means of conservation of biodiversity.

title: Evolution and the Modern World
description: Pandemics, disease, antibiotic resistance, your pets, the food you eat: evolution has shaped our world. Discusses the intellectual development of evolution and illustrates, with current events, that evolution is happening all around us, all the time.

title: Nutrition Science
description: An introduction to nutrition science, integrating concepts from biology, biochemistry, microbiology, physiology, and psychology to explain the interaction between nutrients and the human body. Biological basis of nutrient standards is analyzed. Effects of nutrition, behavior, exercises on health/disease.

title: Science of Cooking
description: The kitchen is used as a laboratory to introduce fundamental principles of biology, chemistry, and physics. A molecular/cellular analysis of cooking, including concepts such as protein structure, browning reactions, colloids, emulsions, carbohydrate metabolism, and development of flavor/texture through biochemical transformations.

title: Global Change Biology
description: Field trips and lectures that address ways in which humans are altering the global environment, with consequences for the ecology of animals, plants, and microbes.

title: Topics in Biological Sciences
description: Studies in selected areas of biological sciences.

title: Molecular Basis of Human Disease
description: Describes the cause and treatment of human diseases at the cutting edge of modern molecular understanding for non-science majors, including history, interviews, and stories.

title: California Teach 1: Introduction to Science and Mathematics Teaching
description: First in a series for students interested in becoming middle or high school teachers of mathematics or science. Students gain an understanding of effective, research-based teaching strategies. Includes supervised field experience in a K-12 classroom.

title: Evolutionary Psychology
description: Introductory overview of the field of evolutionary psychology. Surveys topics operating at the interface of social sciences, i.e. behavioral psychology and evolutionary biology, while developing select aspects of the history of this developing field.

title: Sustainable Landscaping: Design and Practices
description: Through lectures and hands-on work, students learn how to design habitats around dwellings, within cities, and in rural environments. These include traditional/sustainable landscaping, restoration, stormwater/wastewater treatment, xeriscaping, and low impact development design. Sustainable landscape plant materials emphasized.

title: Biology of Cancer
description: Biological, clinical, and psychosocial nature of cancer through the perspectives of medical researchers, biologists, physicians, and health educators. For students of all majors, designed so that each can increase personal awareness of the biology of cancer.

title: The Brain and Behavior
description: Introduction to how the brain works. Biological processes underlying perception, movement, sleep-wake cycles, motivation, language, learning, and memory. Changes in the brain associated with sex differences, drug use, aging, seasons, and time of day. Fundamental properties of the nervous system.

title: Drugs and the Brain
description: Introduction to the actions of drugs on the brain. How studying drug action helps reveal normal functions of neurons. How drugs can correct neural disorders or disrupt neural function. Biological issues related to drug abuse, drug addiction, and drug seeking.

title: Brain Dysfunction and Repair
description: Introduction to the disruptions in brain function that underlie disorders such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinsonism, schizophrenia, and depression, and the basis for drug therapies. The brain's ability to repair itself after damage and the pros and cons of that repair.

title: Mind and Memory
description: Introduction to neural mechanisms underlying learning and memory. Emphasis on molecular changes that mediate memory as well as structures involved in different forms of memory. Additionally, examines the biology of memory phenomena, from extraordinary memory to false memory to amnesia.

title: Mood Disorders
description: There are significant differences in response to psychiatric illness across cultures. Delves into the neuroscience underlying mood disorder, investigating current pharmacological treatments and sociocultural influences on treatment outcomes.

title: AIDS Fundamentals
description: Considers the biological and sociological bases of the AIDS epidemic. Topics include the history of AIDS, current medical knowledge, transmission, risk reduction, and how the community can respond.

title: The Mind-Body Connection in the Neuroscience of Well-Being
description: Explores research that provides insight into how exercise, diet, sleep, contemplative practices such as mindfulness and meditation, and contemporary psychological therapeutic approaches alter brain function in beneficial ways to promote positive mental health well-being.

title: Introduction to Vaccines
description: Focus on introducing vaccines, covering how vaccines work, as well as how they are tested. Introduction to the immune system, to facilitate understanding how vaccines function. Also covers types of vaccines, clinical trials, and vaccine hesitancy.

title: Human Development
description: Processes leading to the birth of a healthy child and the avoidance of birth defects. Male and female reproductive systems, hormonal control of egg-sperm formations, sexual intercourse, contraception, venereal diseases, fertilization, cell division, embryonic development, fetal physiology.

title: Introduction to Biology Research
description: Students use the scientific method, ask and answer questions about environmental DNA, and develop professional skills applicable to any major or career. Focuses on the development of quantitative reasoning, science inquiry, and collaboration skills. Materials Fee

title: From DNA to Organisms
description: Cell biology, biochemistry, genetics, and the biology of organ systems. Covers concepts of building blocks (nucleotides, amino acids, and cells) and of information flow (DNA to proteins, receptors to nuclei, the blood to distant organs, and DNA to offspring).

title: From Organisms to Ecosystems
description: Patterns of diversity, ecology, and evolutionary biology. Emphasis is on the Tree of Life and how its members are distributed and interact.

title: Genetics
description: Introduction to genetics. Basic features of replication and expression of DNA, cell division, and gene transmission. Recombination and mutation in diploid organisms.

title: Biochemistry
description: Structure and properties of proteins; major biochemical pathways and mechanisms for their control.

title: Molecular Biology
description: Biochemistry and replication of nucleic acids; molecular genetics; protein biosynthesis; genetic code; regulation of expression of genetic information; biochemical evolution.

title: Scientific Writing
description: Designed to give an overview of the basic aspects of scientific writing relevant to reporting research in the Biological Sciences.

title: California Teach 2: Middle School Science and Mathematics Teaching
description: Second in a series for students interested in becoming middle or high school teachers of mathematics or science. Students gain an understanding of effective, research-based teaching strategies for grades 6-8. Includes supervised field experience in a middle school classroom.

title: Introduction to STEM Education Research
description: Introduces students to education research and topics studied in STEM education. Enables students to develop research questions, participate in research projects, design and utilize research tools, and contribute to STEM education research products.

title: Research Methods
description: Explores tools of inquiry for developing and implementing science research projects.  Students undertake independent projects requiring data collection, analysis, and modeling, and the organization and presentation of results.  Additional topics include ethical issues and role of scientific literature.

title: Transfer Student Seminar
description: Weekly meetings consisting of presentations by faculty, professional staff, and New Student Peer Academic Advisors provide information about the School of Biological Sciences, campus resources, and special programs/opportunities.

title: Senior Seminar on Global Sustainability I
description: Students attend weekly seminar to discuss current issues in global sustainability. Weekly attendance at Global Sustainability Forum is also required. Seminar utilized to analyze forum presentations.  Prepare bibliography.

title: Senior Seminar on Global Sustainability II
description: Students attend weekly seminar to discuss current issues in global sustainability. Weekly attendance at Global Sustainability Forum is also required. Seminar utilized to analyze forum presentations.  Prepare research proposal.

title: Topics in Biological Sciences Tutoring
description: Tutoring programs with biological sciences student peers.

title: Biological Sciences Learning Assistant Mentoring
description: Oversight of trained learning assistants by the course instructor, including training in the discipline and responsibilities associated with supporting active group work. Instructor mentors LAs in how assisting small groups supports their career goals.

title: Campus as a Living Lab I
description: Students study sustainability concepts and theories and how they apply to our most pressing environmental, social, and economic challenges. Concurrently, they work on hands-on projects to improve the sustainability of campus operations and systems.

title: Campus as a Living Lab II
description: Students study sustainability concepts and theories and how they apply to our most pressing environmental, social, and economic challenges. Concurrently, they work on hands-on projects to improve the sustainability of campus operations and systems.

title: Campus as a Living Lab III
description: Students study sustainability concepts and theories and how they apply to our most pressing environmental, social, and economic challenges. Concurrently, they work on hands-on projects to improve the sustainability of campus operations and systems.

title: Safety and Ethics for Research
description: Introduces students to the concepts, techniques, and ethics involved in biological sciences laboratory work.

title: Special Study in Biological Sciences
description: Individualized instruction dealing with conceptual or theoretical problems in the biological sciences, rather than technical problems.

title: Directed Group Studies
description: Small group experimental laboratory or field work performed under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Independent Study in Biological Sciences Research
description: Individual experimental laboratory or field research under a professor's direction. Required for participation in the Excellence in Research Program.

title: Research Writing
description: Development of research skills such as learning how to plan and conduct research experiments, read and analyze scientific journal articles, and practice presenting the data experiments and journal articles with an emphasis on scientific writing.

title: Topics in Allied Health Microbiology
description: Basic microbiology with emphasis for allied health professions such as nursing. Emphasis is on micro-organisms involved in human health, disease and food safety.

title: Pathways in Biomedical and Translational Science
description: Provides fundamental knowledge to promote understanding of the process of bringing discoveries to the bedside and then to the population, providing a fundamental basis for clinical and translational science.

title: Introduction to Medical Statistics
description: Provides understanding of medical statistics for clinicians; clinical and biomedical researchers to read and interpret literature.

title: Design and Analysis of Clinical Trials
description: Presents history, organization and planning, rationale for methods, limits, and ethics in conducting clinical trials.

title: Science Communication Skills for Clinical and Translational Scientists
description: Designed to train clinical and translational researchers to communicate their research effectively to expert audiences and community members, and creates opportunities for training in scientific writing, presenting scientific talks, or doing job interviews. Recommended elective for all MS-BATS students.

title: Understanding the Pathobiology and Clinical Treatment of Skin Diseases
description: Provides participants with the clinical presentation, biology, and treatment of skin disease. At the completion of the course, attendees are able to identify gaps in research and clinical care of skin diseases.

title: Basic Science of the Skin
description: Provides participants with a basic understanding of the structure and function of skin and the latest advances in skin biology.

title: Biomedical and Translational Science Seminar
description: Students present their current research or a topic of interest and are exposed to diverse projects in the biomedical and translational science arena.

title: Master's Thesis Research and Writing
description: Master’s thesis research and writing with Biomedical and Translational Science faculty.

title: Ethics in Clinical Research
description: Covers major frameworks and concepts of ethics in public health research as well as human subject protection issues.

title: Independent Directed Research
description: Independent research with Biomedical and Translational Science faculty. Capstone option for students who do not choose the thesis option.

title: Introduction to Biomedical Engineering
description: Introduction to the central topics of biomedical engineering. Offers a perspective on bioengineering as a discipline in a seminar format. Principles of problem solving, design, engineering inventiveness, entrepreneurship, information access, communication, ethics, teamwork, and social responsibility are emphasized.

title: Engineering Innovations in Treating Diabetes
description: Innovations in diabetes treatment from the 1800s until the present: purification of insulin, measuring and control of blood glucose, recombinant DNA, clinical trials, and ethics. Solving optimization problems in engineering with Excel.

title: Cell and Molecular Engineering
description: Molecular, structural, genetic, biophysical, and cellular principles of life and bioengineering. Introduction to molecular bioengineering, genetic engineering, synthetic biology, and cell biology. Applications to genetic and biomolecular design.

title: Cell and Molecular Engineering
description: Physiological function from a cellular, molecular, and biophysical perspective. Introduction to genetics, structure and function of cells and tissues, cell cycle control, cancer, and immunology.

title: Engineering Analysis/Design: Data Acquisition
description: Fundamentals of data acquisition programming, basics of computer-based experimentation, establishing interface between computer and data acquisition instrumentation, signal conditioning basics. Materials Fee

title: Engineering Analysis/Design: Data Analysis
description: Overview of MATLAB; numeric, cell, and structure arrays; file management; plotting and model building; solving linear algebraic equations; signal and image processing.  Materials Fee

title: Engineering Analysis/Design: Computer-Aided Design
description: Introduction to SolidWorks and Computer-Aided Design software; design; analysis; rapid prototyping; visualization and presentation; manufacturing planning. Materials Fee

title: Biomechanics I
description: Introduction to statics and dynamics. Topics include rigid bodies, analysis of structures, forces in beams, moments of inertia, friction, kinetics, work, and energy.

title: Biomechanics II
description: Introduction to biomechanics from subcellular to tissue levels. Introduction to stress, strain, and constitutive laws of cells and tissues. Emphasis is placed on biosolids. Introduction to elastic and viscoelastic behaviors with emphasis on the standard linear model of viscoelasticity.

title: Biomechanics III
description: Introduction to human biomechanics with emphasis on cardiovascular biomechanics and biofluid mechanics.

title: Design of Biomaterials
description: Natural and synthetic polymeric materials. Metal and ceramics implant materials. Mechanical properties, degradation, surface characterization, and design of materials. Wound repair, blood clotting, foreign body response, biocompatibility of material. Artificial organs and medical devices. Government regulations.

title: Finite Element Modeling for Bioengineers
description: Introduction to finite-element simulation; fundamentals of Multiphysics simulation; essential skills in performing simulations with COMSOL®.

title: Genetic Engineering and Synthetic Biology
description: Exploring how biological function can be engineered and "synthesized" from the DNA level up.

title: Sensory Motor Systems
description: A quantitative and systems approach to understanding physiological systems. Systems covered include the nervous and musculoskeletal systems.

title: Quantitative Physiology:  Organ Transport Systems
description: A quantitative and systems approach to understanding physiological systems. Systems covered include the cardiopulmonary, circulatory, and renal systems.

title: Biomedical Signals and Systems
description: Analysis of analog and digital biomedical signals; Fourier Series expansions; difference and differential equations; convolutions. System models: discrete-time and continuous-time linear time-invariant systems; Laplace and Fourier transforms. Analysis of signals and systems using computer programs.

title: Introduction to Computational Biology
description: The use of theories and methods based on computer science, mathematics, and physics in molecular biology and biochemistry. Basics in biomolecular modeling. Analysis of sequence and structural data of biomolecules. Analysis of biomolecular functions.

title: Photomedicine
description: Studies the use of optical and engineering-based systems (laser-based) for diagnosis, treating diseases, manipulation of cells and cell function. Physical, optical, and electro-optical principles are explored regarding molecular, cellular, organ, and organism applications.

title: Engineering Medical Optics
description: Principles of optics and photonics, integration of optical components into systems and devices, and analysis of physiological signals obtained from Biophotonics measurements.

title: Introduction to Biomedical Imaging
description: Introduction to imaging modalities widely used in medicine and biology, including X-ray, computed tomography (CT), nuclear medicine (PET and SPET), ultrasonic imaging, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), optical tomography, imaging contrast, imaging processing, and complementary nature of the imaging modalities.

title: Spectroscopy and Imaging of Biological Systems
description: Principles of spectroscopy; absorption; molecular orbitals; multiphoton transitions; Jablonski diagram; fluorescence anisotropy; fluorescence decay; quenching; FRET; excited state reactions; solvent relaxations; instruments; microscopy: wide field, LSM, TPE; fluorescent probes, fluctuations spectroscopy; optical resolution and super-resolution; CARS and SHG microscopy.

title: Design of Biomedical Electronics
description: Analog and digital circuits in bioinstrumentation. AC and DC circuit analysis, design and construction of filter and amplifiers using operational amplifier, digitization of signals and data acquisition, bioelectrical signals, design and construction of ECG instrument, bioelectrical signal measurement and analysis.  Materials Fee

title: Microfabrication
description: Essentials of photolithography, soft-lithography, microfabrication, Microelectromechanical Systems (MEMS), BioMEMS; applications in biomedical engineering.

title: Microfluidics and Lab-on-a-Chip
description: Introduction to principles of microfluidics and state-of-the-art micro Total Analysis Systems (uTAS). Lab-on-a-Chip for bimolecular assays with device design principles for microscale sample preparation, flow transport, bimolecular manipulation, separation and detection, and the technologies for integrating these devices into microsystems.

title: Microimplants
description: Essential concepts of biomedical implants at the micro scale. Design, fabrication, and applications of several microimplantable devices including cochlear, retinal, neural, and muscular implants.

title: Biotransport Phenomena
description: Fundamentals of heat and mass transfer, similarities in the respective rate equations. Emphasis on practical application of fundamental principles.

title: Tissue Engineering
description: Quantitative analysis of cell and tissue functions. Emerging developments in stem cell technology, biodegradable scaffolds, growth factors, and others important in developing clinical products. Applications of bioengineering.

title: Introduction to Modern Bioengineering Tools
description: A broad picture of diverse modern bioengineering tools, including genetic engineering and its risks to society, how to sequence DNA/RNA molecules with single base resolution, how to manipulate small biomolecules one at a time.

title: Biomedical Engineering Laboratory
description: Measurement and analysis of biological systems using engineering tools and techniques. Laboratory experiments involve living systems with the emphasis on measuring physiological parameters. Materials Fee

title: Biomedical Engineering Design: Addressing Unmet Clinical Needs
description: Introduction to unmet clinical needs identification and evaluation in biomedical engineering design.

title: Biomedical Engineering Design
description: Design strategies, techniques, tools, and protocols commonly encountered in biomedical engineering; industrial design experience in group projects; ethics, economic analysis, and FDA product approval. Materials Fee

title: Biomedical Engineering Design
description: Design strategies, techniques, tools, and protocols commonly encountered in biomedical engineering; industrial design experience in group projects; ethics, economic analysis, and FDA product approval. Materials Fee

title: Biomedical Engineering Design
description: Design strategies, techniques, tools, and protocols commonly encountered in biomedical engineering; industrial design experience in group projects; ethics, economic analysis, and FDA product approval. Materials Fee

title: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering
description: Studies in selected areas of Biomedical Engineering. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Seminars in Biomedical Engineering
description: Presentation of advanced topics and reports of current research efforts in Biomedical Engineering.

title: Biomedical Engineering Internship
description: Students majoring in BME may receive credit for an approved internship, working at a company under the supervision of an industry mentor and a faculty advisor. Enables students to gain valuable experience in a professional setting and enhance their skills.

title: Individual Study
description: Independent research conducted in the lab of a biomedical engineering core faculty member. A formal written report of the research conducted is required at the conclusion of the quarter.

title: Individual Study
description: Supervised independent reading, research, or design for undergraduate Engineering majors. Students taking individual study for design credit are to submit a written paper to the instructor and to the Undergraduate Student Affairs Office in the School of Engineering.

title: Biomedical Big Data
description: Analysis and visualization of large biomedical datasets. Topics covered include cloud computing, learning Structured Query Language (SQL), database normalization and joins, using Google’s BigQuery, using the statistical analysis package R, machine learning algorithms, application of machine learning for classification problems.

title: Biomedical Imaging and Biophotonics
description: Designed as a subfield of optical imaging and biophotonics as it applies to their applications to basic lifesciences and in vivo imaging to diagnose disease. Topics span all of of the areas of biophotonics and techniques.

title: Digital Health
description: Digital health is the convergence of genomics and technology to radically change the way health and medicine is practiced. Explores the history of healthcare in the U.S., its current status, and the future of digital health.

title: Molecular and Cellular Engineering
description: Engineering of physiological function at the genetic, cellular, and tissue scales. Topics include cloning and genetic engineering, extracellular matrix biomaterials, principles of regenerative medicine and tissue engineering, and experimental design.

title: Molecular and Cellular Engineering
description: Engineering of physiological function at the genetic, cellular, and tissue scales. Topics include cloning and genetic engineering, extracellular matrix biomaterials, principles of regenerative medicine and tissue engineering, and experimental design.

title: Microscale Tissue Engineering
description: Engineering of physiological function at the scale of individual cells. Topics include cell micropatterning, microfluidic tissue culture, engineering the cellular microenvironment, and microphysiological systems.

title: Microscale Tissue Engineering
description: Engineering of physiological function at the scale of individual cells. Topics include cell micropatterning, microfluidic tissue culture, engineering the cellular microenvironment, and microphysiological systems.

title: Cardiovascular Mechanobiology
description: Advanced topics in cellular engineering and mechanobiology, with focus on the cardiovascular system.  Tools and techniques used to manipulate and measure mechanical forces at the molecular, cellular, tissue, and organ levels, and their applications in cardiovascular devices and tissue engineering.

title: Cardiovascular Mechanobiology
description: Advanced topics in cellular engineering and mechanobiology, with focus on the cardiovascular system. Tools and techniques used to manipulate and measure mechanical forces at the molecular, cellular, tissue, and organ levels, and their applications in cardiovascular devices and tissue engineering.

title: Systems Cell and Developmental Biology
description: Introduces concepts needed to understand cell and developmental biology at the systems level, i.e., how the parts (molecules) work together to create a complex output. Emphasis on using mathematical/computational modeling to expand/modify insights provided by intuition.

title: Linking Modeling and Experiments in Bioengineering
description: Overview of modeling based on experimental techniques in bioengineering. Construct and evaluate models of varying complexity and to relate them to experimental data.

title: Sensory Motor Systems
description: A quantitative and systems approach to understanding physiological systems. Systems covered include the nervous and musculoskeletal systems.

title: Sensory Motor Systems
description: A quantitative and systems approach to understanding physiological systems. Systems covered include the nervous and musculoskeletal systems.

title: Organ Transport Systems
description: A quantitative and systems approach to understanding physiological systems. Systems covered include the cardiopulmonary, circulatory, and renal systems.

title: Organ Transport Systems
description: Applies engineering models and mathematics to understand human physiology in order to understand the physiology. Covers the pulmonary, cardiac, and cardiovascular systems focusing on transport phenomena.

title: Biofluid Mechanics
description: Introduces principles of biofluid mechanics in a research oriented scheme and approaches a wide spectrum of biofluid related problems in human body and solutions that involves engineering concepts.

title: Applied Engineering Mathematics I
description: Analytical techniques applied to engineering problems in transport phenomena, process dynamics and control, and thermodynamics.

title: Applied Engineering Mathematics II
description: Focuses on biomedical system identification. Includes fundamental techniques of model building and testing such as formulation, solution of governing equations, sensitivity theory, identifiability theory, and uncertainty analysis.

title: Introduction to Computational Biology
description: The use of theories and methods based on computer science, mathematics, and physics in molecular biology and biochemistry. Basics in biomolecular modeling. Analysis of sequence and structural data of biomolecules. Analysis of biomolecular functions.

title: Dynamic Systems in Biology and Medicine
description: Introduces principles of system theory to analyze biological, biochemical, physiological, and bioengineering systems. Analytical and computational tools are used to model and analyze dynamic systems such as population, neuronal and heart dynamics, biochemical and physiological systems, oxygen diffusion and similar.

title: Dynamic Systems in Biology and Medicine
description: Introduces the elements of system theory, and applies these principles to analyze biomedical, chemical, social, and engineering systems. Using analytical and computational tools to model and analyze various dynamic systems.

title: Neuroimaging Data Analysis
description: Knowledge and understanding of recent techniques for the analysis of healthy and pathological structure and function in neuroimaging data.

title: Neuroimaging Data Analysis
description: Knowledge and understanding of recent techniques for the analysis of healthy and pathological structure and function in neuroimaging data.

title: Analysis of Neural Time Series
description: Hands-on introduction to techniques for the analysis of neural time series data, with a primary focus on the electroencephalogram (EEG). Topics may include the physiological basis of EEG, time-frequency analysis, spatial filtering, and methods of assessing connectivity.

title: Spectroscopy and Imaging of Biological Systems
description: Principles of spectroscopy; absorption; molecular orbitals; multiphoton transitions; Jablonski diagram; fluorescence anisotropy; fluorescence decay; quenching; FRET; excited state reactions; solvent relaxations; instruments; microscopy: wide field, LSM, TPE; fluorescent probes, fluctuations spectroscopy; optical resolution and super-resolution; CARS and SHG microscopy.

title: Spectroscopy and Imaging of Biological Systems
description: Principles of spectroscopy; absorption; molecular orbitals; multiphoton transitions; Jablonski diagram; fluorescence anisotropy; fluorescence decay; quenching; FRET; excited state reactions; solvent relaxations; instruments; microscopy: wide field, LSM, TPE; fluorescent probes, fluctuations spectroscopy; optical resolution and super-resolution; CARS and SHG microscopy.

title: Introduction to Clinical Medicine for Biomedical Engineering
description: An introduction to clinical medicine for graduate students in biomedical engineering. Lectures and rotations through nephology, gastroenterology, pulmonary, and critical care cardiology.

title: Introduction to Clinical Medicine for Biomedical Engineering
description: An introduction to clinical medicine for graduate students in biomedical engineering. Lectures and rotations through nephology, gastroenterology, pulmonary, and critical care cardiology.

title: Engineering Medical Optics
description: Principles of optics and photonics, integration of optical components into systems and devices, and analysis of physiologic signals obtained from Biophotonics measurements.

title: Engineering Medical Optics
description: Principles of optics and photonics, integration of optical components into systems and devices, and analysis of physiologic signals obtained from Biophotonics measurements.

title: Cardiovascular Devices
description: Cardiovascular implants, the science around those, regulatory affairs, and cardiovascular device market and related business opportunities. Students form practice startups around a clinical unmet need and use their knowledge from the course to plan it accordingly.

title: Microfluids and Lab-on-a-Chip
description: Introduction to microfluidics and state-of-the-art micro Total Analysis Systems (uTAS). Lab-on-a-Chip for biomolecular assays with device design principles for microscale sample preparation, flow transport, biolmolecular manipulation, separation and detection, and the technologies for integrating these devices into microsystems.

title: Microimplants
description: Essential concepts of biomedical implants at the micro scale. Design, fabrication, and applications of several microimplantable devices including cochlear, retinal, neural, and muscular implants.

title: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering
description: Studies in selected areas of Biomedical Engineering. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering
description: Studies in selected areas of Biomedical Engineering. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Master of Science Thesis Research
description: Individual research or investigation conducted in the pursuit of preparing and completing the thesis required for the M.S. in Engineering.

title: Doctor of Philosophy Dissertation Research
description: Individual research or investigation conducted in the pursuit of preparing and completing the dissertation required for the Ph.D. in Engineering.

title: Seminars in Biomedical Engineering
description: Presentation of advanced topics and reports of current research efforts in biomedical engineering. Designed for graduate students in the Biomedical Engineering program.

title: Individual Research
description: Individual research or investigation under the direction of an individual faculty.

title: Introduction to Chemical Engineering
description: Helps new students successfully transition to UCI, build a sense of community in the department, develop networking and professional skills, and introduce a variety of industries who employ chemical engineers through a series of guest lectures.

title: Chemical Processes and Material Balances
description: Introduction to chemical engineering and the industries where chemical engineers play vital roles. Problem-solving skills and techniques. Quantitative calculations and applications using mass and energy balances. Stoichiometric equations, multiple bypasses, and others in process industries.

title: Process Thermodynamics
description: Principles of thermodynamics: definitions, basic concepts, and laws; property relationships; construction of thermodynamic charts and tables; energy balances; phase and chemical equilibria; combined mass and energy balances.

title: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Elements of chemical engineering thermodynamics, including equilibrium and stability; equations of state; generalized correlations of properties of materials; properties of ideal and non-ideal mixtures; thermodynamics of real solutions; ideal and non-ideal phase equilibria; chemical equilibria for ideal and non-ideal solutions.

title: Introduction to Numerical Methods in Engineering
description: An introduction to the fundamentals of numerical analysis and the computer algorithms in MATLAB for the solution of engineering problems, with emphasis on problems arising in chemical engineering thermodynamics, transport phenomena, and reaction engineering.

title: Engineering Physical Chemistry
description: Provides an integrated view of both classical and molecular perspectives on thermodynamics, thermodynamic potentials, entropy, and the second law. Students learn how to use statistical mechanics to create a bridge between the quantum mechanical world and the familiar macroscopic one.

title: Reaction Kinetics and Reactor Design
description: Introduction to quantitative analysis of chemical reactions and chemical reactor design. Reactor operations including batch, continuous stirred tank, and tubular reactor. Homogeneous and heterogeneous reactions.

title: Momentum Transfer
description: Fluid statics, surface tension, Newton's law of viscosity, non-Newtonian and complex flows, momentum equations, laminar and turbulent flow, velocity profiles, flow in pipes and around objects, piping systems design, pumps and mixing, and other applications to chemical and related industries.

title: Heat Transfer
description: Principles of conduction, radiation, and convection of heat; phenomenological rate laws, differential and macroscopic energy balances; heat transfer rates, steady state and unsteady state conduction, convection; applications to chemical and related industries.

title: Mass Transfer
description: Molecular and continuum approaches to diffusion and convection in multi-component mixtures; steady state, quasi-steady state and transient mass transfer; effect of reactions on mass transfer; convective mass transfer; simultaneous mass, heat and momentum transfer; applications to chemical and related industries.

title: Separation Processes
description: Application of equilibria and mass and energy balances for design of separation processes. Use of equilibrium laws for design of distillation, absorption, stripping, and extraction equipment. Design of multicomponent separators.

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory I
description: Experimental study of thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, and heat and mass transfer. Operation and evaluation of process equipment, data analysis.  Materials Fee

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory II
description: Continuation of the CBE 140A covering mass transfer operations such as distillation, absorption, extraction, etc. Rate and equilibria studies in simple chemical systems with and without reaction. Study of chemical process. Materials Fee

title: Chemical Process Control
description: Dynamic responses and control of chemical process equipment, dynamic modeling of chemical processes, linear system analysis, analyses and design of feedback loops and advanced control systems.

title: Chemical Engineering Design I
description: Introduction to process design; flow sheets for chemical processes; synthesis of multicomponent separation sequences and reaction paths; synthesis of heat exchange networks; computer-aided design and simulation of processes and components pacts.

title: Chemical Engineering Design II
description: Application of chemical engineering basics to practical design problems; process economics; process safety; environmental impacts; a major team design project with progress reports, oral presentation, and technical report with engineering drawings and economics.

title: Engineering Biology
description: First-principle introduction to the modern biochemistry, molecular biology, and cell biology with an engineering language. The goal is to demonstrate that the vastly diverse biological phenomena can be explained by a set of fundamental principles in chemistry, thermodynamics, and kinetics.

title: Introduction to Biochemical Engineering
description: Application of engineering principles to biochemical processes. Topics include microbial pathways, energetics and control systems, enzyme and microbial kinetics, and the design and analysis of biological reactors.

title: Kinetics of Biochemical Networks
description: Principles from statistical mechanics, thermodynamics, and chemical kinetics applied to biochemical systems, from fundamental processes such as receptor-ligand binding and enzyme catalysis, to complex cellular functions including signal transduction and gene regulation.

title: Applied Spectroscopy
description: Designed to teach fundamental principles of spectroscopy and their application to photon and electron spectroscopies of the gas and condensed phases. Covers theoretical principles of rotational, vibrational, and electronic spectroscopies. Specific topics include infrared and Raman spectroscopies, Auger, X-ray, etc.

title: Electrochemical Engineering
description: Introduction to fundamentals of electrochemical engineering and its application to variety of energy conversion- and storage-technologies. Planned topics include: electric double layers, reaction kinetics, transport, batteries, fuel-cells.

title: Nuclear and Radiochemistry
description: Advanced treatment of nuclear structure, nuclear reactions, and radioactive-decay processes. Introduction to nuclear activation analysis, isotope effects, radiation chemistry, hot-atom chemistry, nuclear age-dating methods, nuclear reactors, and nuclear power.

title: Polymer Science and Engineering
description: An introduction to physical aspects of polymers, including configuration and conformation of polymer chains and characterization techniques; crystallinity, viscoelasticity, mechanical properties, polymer alloys, processing, and application.

title: Surface and Adhesion Science
description: Structure, thermodynamics of, kinetics, and reactions on surfaces. Surface electronic and mechanical properties and characterization of all classes of materials including metals, semiconductors, ceramics, polymers, and soft materials. Adhesion between different materials is also addressed.

title: Special Topics in Chemical Engineering
description: Studies in selected areas of Chemical Engineering. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering Internship
description: Students majoring in CBE may receive credit for an approved internship, working at a company under the supervision of an industry mentor and a faculty advisor. Enables students to gain valuable experience in a professional setting and enhance their skills.

title: Group Study
description: Group study of selected topics in engineering.

title: Individual Study
description: For undergraduate engineering majors in supervised but independent readings, research, or design. Students taking individual study for design credit are to submit a written paper to the instructor and to the Undergraduate Student Affairs Office in the School of Engineering.

title: Individual Study
description: For undergraduate Engineering majors in supervised but independent reading, research, or design. Students taking individual study for design credit are to submit a written paper to the instructor and to the Undergraduate Student Affairs Office in The School of Engineering.

title: Applied Engineering Mathematics I
description: Analytical techniques applied to engineering problems in transport phenomena, process dynamics and control, and thermodynamics.

title: Reaction Engineering
description: Advanced topics in reaction engineering, reactor stability analysis, diffusional effect in heterogeneous catalysis, energy balance, optimization of reactor operation, dispersed in phase reactors.

title: Batteries I: Fundamentals
description: Batteries are electrochemical energy storage devices. Batteries are found in mobile devices as well as transportation or stationary applications. Introduces fundamentals of batteries and organic electrochemistry, operation principles, device limitations, and power densities.

title: Transport Phenomena I
description: Continuum modeling and analysis of heat and mass transfer from an integrated viewpoint. Scaling concepts, relevance of characteristic time- and length-scales, steady- and unsteady-state processes, bounded and unbounded problems, and convective transport, with applications to chemical and biological processes.

title: Advanced Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Introduction to modern thermodynamics and applications, with a focus on aspects relevant to chemical and materials engineering. Mathematical tools; equilibrium and stability; microscope rigorous equations of state; molecular-level thermodynamics of real mixtures; and phase and chemical equilibrium.

title: Electrolyzers: Fundamentals and Applications
description: Hydrogen has potential to decarbonize many sectors, including grid, transportation, chemicals production, and agriculture. Electrolysis is a process where electricity is used to produce hydrogen by splitting water, with no resulting carbon emissions. Several types of electrolyzers are covered.

title: Special Topics in Chemical Engineering
description: Studies in selected areas of Chemical Engineering. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Research Methods and Technical Communication
description: Intended for Ph.D. students to develop critical research skills in creating archival papers, intellectual property and technical proposals, and in analysis of the scientific literature.

title: Molecular Biotechnology
description: Engineering and biological principles important in recombinant cell technology. Host/vector selection; plasmid propagation; optimization of cloned gene expression; metabolic engineering; protein secretion; experimental techniques; modeling of recombinant cell systems.

title: Metabolic Engineering and Synthetic Biology
description:
Synthesis of chemicals from renewable carbon and energy sources using
metabolically engineered biological systems represents one of the most
promising solutions to the global problems of energy crisis and climate change.

title: Drug Delivery
description: Introduction to design of drug delivery systems. Includes physicochemical and pharmacokinetic considerations in drug formulations, types of therapeutics, routes of administration, biomaterials, and novel drug delivery systems.

title: Bioseparation Processes
