title: Financial Research Methodology
description: Provides an introduction into the computational research methods necessary in modern finance, such as optimization, simulation methods, and finite difference and finite element methods. Some applications include quadratic programming for portfolio selection and option pricing.

title: Risk Management
description: Examines modern techniques for managing financial risks, including market, credit, liquidity, and operational risks. Covers measurement techniques for different financial risks and instruments. Discusses how risk measurement tools can be used for management of the risk/return profile of financial institutions.

title: Portfolio Management
description: Provides a working understanding of the themes and concepts of portfolio management. Covers several disciplines, such as investment valuation, investment decision-making, investing styles, asset allocation, monitoring and re-balancing portfolios, risk management strategies, performance evaluation, and attribution analysis.

title: Bonds and Fixed Income
description: Addresses concepts covering a broad spectrum of debt securities with emphasis on qualitative and quantitative analysis of fixed income securities in many areas, including corporate (credit) debt, MBS/ABS, structured debt, and credit derivatives.

title: Multinational Finance
description: Examines the history of the world’s foreign exchange markets, the macroeconomics of foreign exchange, major parity conditions, hedging of currency exposure and international investment. Gives students a basic understanding of foreign exchange market.

title: FinTech
description: Provides working knowledge of the role of IT-enabled innovation in financial markets to help students acquire an understanding of broad economic issues that are of wide applicability in many areas of FinTech. Both qualitative analysis and quantitative applications are explored.

title: The Real Estate Development Process
description: Nature and composition of development community and development process. Emphasis on role of conflicting interests, values and goals, and market uncertainty. Special attention paid to deal structuring and risk management. Lectures, guest lecturers, team projects evaluating actual development project.

title: Seminar in Management of the Real Estate Enterprise
description: Capstone seminar. Looks beyond “The Deal” to management of the real estate enterprise itself. Explores aspects of decision-making focused upon strategic objectives: goal setting, legal/tax structures, public vs. closely-held entities, corporate ethics, capital structure, managing growth, diversification, technology. Guest professionals.

title: Real Estate Transactions
description: For real estate professionals engaged in purchasing, selling, appraising, evaluating, analyzing and lending on, and developing real property with the basic tools needed to understand the primary elements of a commercial real estate transaction in California.

title: Corporate Real Estate Management (CREM)
description: Introduction to Corporate Real Estate Management (CREM) and its many facets. Appreciate the role of CREM, its impact on global society, and its importance to both the real estate community and corporations that it supports.

title: Cases in Corporate Financial Management
description: Case-based course in corporate finance. Introduces advanced analytical tools and techniques for value-based financial management in a broad range of corporate settings, including financial forecasting, mergers and acquisitions, leveraged buyouts, ownership and control, corporate restructuring, and financial distress.

title: Derivatives
description: Covers major derivative instruments that are widely used for hedging, including forward, futures, swaps and options, speculation, and arbitrage. Focuses on understanding the instruments, how to use and price them, basic risk management principles, and how to implement investment strategies.

title: Mergers and Acquisitions
description: Focuses on mastering mergers and acquisitions concepts and techniques that enable participants to practically apply them in related fields such as investment banking, consulting, and corporate development. Studies drivers of success in M&A with heavy attention on transactional review.

title: Wealth Management
description: Provides overview of considerations needed to connect singular functional areas that comprise personal and business wealth management, including basic and advanced estate planning, investment management, tax management, life/liability and property/casualty insurance, medical and long‐term care coverage, retirement and philanthropy considerations.

title: Venture Capital and Private Equity
description: Overview of venture capital and private equity, which constitute the “alternative” asset class (as opposed to conventional investments). Many start-up and entrepreneurial firms require substantial capital, and venture capital and private equity organizations finance these high-risk but potentially high-reward companies.

title: Finance Internship
description: Provides students with opportunities to apply knowledge obtained from classroom theory through actual practical work experiences. Also provides knowledge of career opportunities, understanding of finance in business environments, and preparation for the finance profession.

title: Special Topics in Finance
description: Studies in selected areas of finance. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Master of Finance Capstone
description: Students work in teams on an applied finance project associated with their area of focus to demonstrate knowledge and expertise. The project can involve experiential learning, collaboration with a faculty member, or association with an internship.

title: Individual Study
description: Individual study under the direction of a select faculty member.

title: Fundamentals of French
description: First quarter of first-year French in a hybrid format. Students are taught to conceptualize in French as they learn to understand, read, write, and speak. Classes are conducted entirely in French.

title: Fundamentals of French
description: Second quarter of first-year French in a hybrid format. Students are taught to conceptualize in French as they learn to understand, read, write, and speak. Classes are conducted entirely in French.

title: Fundamentals of French
description: Third quarter of first-year French in a hybrid format. Students are taught to conceptualize in French as they learn to understand, read, write, and speak. Classes are conducted entirely in French.

title: Intermediate French
description: Second-year French in a hybrid format. Students are taught to conceptualize in French as they learn to understand, read, write, and speak. Classes are conducted entirely in French.

title: Intermediate French
description: Second-year French in a hybrid format. Students are taught to conceptualize in French as they learn to understand, read, write, and speak. Classes are conducted entirely in French.

title: Intermediate French
description: Second-year French in a hybrid format. Students are taught to conceptualize in French as they learn to understand, read, write, and speak. Classes are conducted entirely in French.

title: French Peer Tutoring
description: Tutoring Program in which advanced French students provide assistance to students at a lower level. One hour of tutoring per week.

title: Topics in French Culture and the Modern World
description: Introductory course for non-majors. Focuses on France's role in the modern world and its cultural connections to Asia, the Middle East, Africa, Europe, and the Americas. Taught in English.

title: Grammar and Composition
description: Review of grammar taught in FRENCH 2A -B-C. Students gain facility in writing French and increase reading comprehension. Short texts and films are introduced to generate substantive discussion, and multiple short writing exercises are assigned to solidify skills. Formerly FRENCH 60.

title: Topics in Issues in French and Francophone Culture
description: Investigation of an issue of cultural significance in the francophone world through historical documents, literary works, and newspaper articles. Supplemented with films and/or other elements of popular culture. Multiple short writing assignments to solidify writing skills. Formerly FRENCH 61.

title: Translation
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of translation from French to English and English to French. Many opportunities provided to improve skills in writing and comprehension. Formerly FRENCH 62.

title: Topics in the Work World in French
description: A view into the world of work in French. Areas of study include international relations; French administration; and tensions between management and workers. Attention given to vocabulary and grammar of professional interactions in French. Formerly FRENCH 63.

title: Advanced French Language and Style
description: Intended for advanced students who wish to improve their expressive capabilities in French. Review of more nuanced grammar points. Emphasis placed on rhetoric, syntax, and precision. Advanced readings increase comprehension. Formerly FRENCH 64.

title: French for International Relations
description: Explores the culture of international relations, diplomacy, and humanitarian organizations in France and the French-speaking world with an emphasis on oral communication usually through the completion of simulated professional tasks in a professional context.

title: Topics in Medieval Literature and Culture
description: Examines the literature and culture of the Middle Ages with attention to the historical context of feudalism and the early development of the French language.

title: Topics in 16th-Century French Literature
description: Examines the diverse literature of the Renaissance and the Age of Exploration.

title: Topics in 17th-Century French Literature
description: Examines the age of drama and other vibrant forms in French literature of the period.

title: Topics in 18th-Century French Literature
description: Examines the literature and philosophy of the Enlightenment, the Ancient Régime, Classicism, and/or Revolution.

title: Topics in 19th-Century French Literature
description: Focuses on the literature of an era that experienced many modernist transformations.

title: Topics in 20th-Century French and Francophone Literature
description: A study of modern and contemporary literature and culture.

title: Topics in Francophone Literature and Culture
description: Literature and cultures of the francophone world.

title: Literature and Society
description: In English. Readings of masterpieces of French literature in their social, political, and historical contexts. Requires at least 4,000 words of assigned composition based on French works. Several essays required.

title: Topics in French Literary Genre
description: Examines the development and transformation of a single genre, such as the poem or the novel.

title: Topics in French Literature and Culture
description: Study of a theme, movement, or problem crucial to understanding French Literature and Culture.

title: French Cinema
description: Study of a period, movement, or theme in French or Francophone cinema.

title: Topics in History and Literature
description: Examines the dialogue between historical events and literary texts.

title: Politics and Literature
description: Examines the role played by politics and ethics in French literature, film, and culture.

title: Special Studies in French
description: A project proposal is prepared by the student and approved by the faculty member who directs the project. Procedure must be completed by the end of the first week of classes.

title: Studies in Theory and Criticism
description: Advanced study of a topic in theory and/or criticism.

title: Dissertation Research
description: For graduate students writing a dissertation with a faculty member in French.

title: University Teaching
description: For graduate students completing a course on foreign language pedagogy.

title: Fundamentals of German
description: Emphasizes the development of meaningful communicative skills in German for the purposes of interaction with German speakers and beginning study of German. With a learner-centered approach, the courses help students develop speaking, listening, reading, writing, and cultural skills and knowledge.

title: Fundamentals of German
description: Emphasizes the development of meaningful communicative skills in German for the purposes of interaction with German speakers and beginning study of German. With a learner-centered approach,  students develop speaking, listening, reading, writing, and cultural skills and knowledge.

title: Fundamentals of German
description: Emphasizes the development of meaningful communicative skills in German for the purposes of interaction with German speakers and beginning study of German. With a learner-centered approach, students develop speaking, listening, reading, writing, and cultural skills and knowledge.

title: Intermediate German
description: Emphasizes communicative skills for the purposes of interaction with German speakers and intermediate study of German. With a learner-centered approach, helps students develop reading, writing, speaking, listening, grammatical, and cultural skills and knowledge. First-year grammar is reviewed and expanded.

title: Intermediate German
description: Emphasizes communicative skills for the purposes of interaction with German speakers and intermediate study of German. With a learner-centered approach, helps students develop reading, writing, speaking, listening, grammatical, and cultural skills and knowledge. First-year grammar is reviewed and expanded.

title: Intermediate German
description: Emphasizes communicative skills for the purposes of interaction with German speakers and intermediate study of German. With a learner-centered approach, helps students develop reading, writing, speaking, listening, grammatical, and cultural skills and knowledge. First-year grammar is reviewed and expanded.

title: Fundamentals of German (with Emphasis on Reading)
description: Primarily for students interested in acquiring a solid reading knowledge of German. Facilitates comprehension and translation of texts in various disciplines. Does not serve as prerequisite for any higher-level German courses or fulfill any undergraduate foreign language requirement.

title: Topics in Introduction to German Literature and Culture
description: Sample interpretations of texts in their cultural and historical contexts. Introduction to critical language in German.

title: Topics in German Culture and Society
description: Interdisciplinary introduction to German culture from the perspective of its aesthetic, social, and political aspects. Methodological problems arising from an analysis of culture in its historical context.

title: Topics in German Film
description: Introduction to the history and interpretation of German film within its cultural and social contexts. Enhances German grammar knowledge and vocabulary and develops sophisticated speaking, writing, and reading skills.

title: Topics in Advanced German for Business and Economics
description: Explores the structure of the German economy and business practices while developing advanced verbal and written skills important for professional life in Germany. Taught in German.

title: Topics in Literary Theory and Criticism
description: In English. Theoretical dimensions of literary criticism and the German philosophical tradition. Topics have included Marxism, Freudian thought, German Idealist aesthetics, Historicism, twentieth-century hermeneutics, Frankfurt School, and Rezeptionsaesthetik.

title: German Literature and Culture in Translation
description: In English. Major works in Germanic literature and culture in context.

title: German Cinema
description: Historical, theoretical, and comparative perspectives on German cinema.

title: German Cinema
description: Historical, theoretical, and comparative perspectives on German cinema.

title: Topics in German Linguistics
description: Explores linguistic, sociolinguistic, or ethnography-of-communication topics of German or other Germanic languages (Swedish, Icelandic, Yiddish, and others). Taught in English.

title: German Internship
description: In this internship course, students will engage in professional practice (e.g., Engineering, Business Administration, government) in a German setting and thereby increase their knowledge of German language and culture in a setting beyond the academic classroom.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent research with German faculty.

title: Literary Theory
description: Topics in literary theory.

title: Literary and Cultural History
description: Topics in literary and cultural history.

title: Independent Study
description: Counted toward course requirements for the M.A. or Ph.D. A term paper or project is required.

title: Independent Directed Reading
description: For students preparing for doctoral examination.

title: Dissertation Research
description: For students who have been admitted to doctoral candidacy.

title: University Teaching
description: Limited to Teaching Assistants.

title: Social Media and Social Change
description: Explores social media’s role in activism, identity formation, and global movements. Students analyze digital campaigns, online rhetoric, and cultural impacts while integrating theoretical perspectives with real-world case studies to develop critical media literacy and digital engagement skills.

title: Global Cultures I
description: Introduction to the processes by which economies, cultural practices, national entities, groups, individuals, and personal identities have undergone globalization. General background and methodological tools for understanding problems and processes of globalization.

title: Global Cultures II
description: Introduction to the processes by which economies, cultural practices, national entities, groups, individuals, and personal identities have undergone globalization. Explores how globalization has manifested itself in specific topics, periods, or societies.

title: Language Origins: Evolution, Genetics, and the Brain
description: Examines how human language(s) may have originated. Studies pertinent techniques (reconstruction) and addresses related questions, including is our language faculty inborn (i.e., genetically encoded)? Can brain imaging and population genetics research help to unlock this mystery of human evolution?

title: Senior Seminar:Topics in Global Cultures
description: Students explore a topic(s) concerning processes and/or problems of globalization from an interdisciplinary perspective and build on their critical and analytical skills when investigating cultural and other phenomena that cut across national borders. Research assignments, class presentations, final seminar paper.

title: Independent Study
description: Directed reading and research in consultation with a faculty advisor. Substantial written work required.

title: Classical and Biblical Greek
description: Elements of Classical Greek grammar, syntax, and vocabulary.

title: Classical and Biblical Greek
description: Elements of Classical Greek grammar, syntax, and vocabulary.

title: Classical and Biblical Greek
description: Introduction to reading texts.

title: Special Studies in Greek
description: Lower-division level independent research with Greek faculty.

title: Topics in  Attic Greek
description: Selected readings in Attic Greek prose and poetry. Authors may include Xenophon, Lysias, Antiphon, Euripides, and others. Selections from prose and poetry will be read each quarter. Grammar and syntax review included.

title: Seminar in Greek Prose
description: Specialized and focused study of a particular Greek prose author or topic.

title: Seminar in Greek Poetry
description: Specialized and focused study of a particular topic or author of Greek poetry.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Special topics in Greek culture and civilization through directed reading and research.

title: Independent Studies in Greek
description: Independent research with Greek faculty.

title: Humanities and Arts: Problems and Methods for Global Middle East Studies
description: Introduces students to the broad set of humanities and arts approaches to studying the Middle East as a global zone of cultural, political, and economic interaction.

title: Social Sciences: Problems and Methods for Global Middle East Studies
description: Introduces students to the broad set of social sciences approaches to studying the Middle East as a global zone of cultural, political, and economic interaction.

title: Social Ecology and Sciences: Problems and Methods for Global Middle East Studies
description: Introduces students to the broad set of approaches to studying the Middle East as a global zone of cultural, political, and economic interaction, focusing on the disciplines related to Social Ecology.

title: Research and Writing for Global Middle East Studies
description: Research and writing course for Global Middle East Studies majors. The primary focus is to refine the skills necessary for students to engage in independent research and writing in Global Middle East Studies.

title: Fundamentals of Hebrew
description: Designed for students with little or no exposure to Hebrew. May focus on Biblical Hebrew and/or modern Hebrew. Students learn the writing system and basics of grammar via latest pedagogical materials. Topics in Jewish culture are also included.

title: Hebrew Peer-to-Peer Language Mentor Program
description: Peer-to-Peer Language Mentor Program in which student mentors work with student mentees to increase the language skills of both participants. One hour weekly meetings. HEBREW 10A is for language mentors.

title: Hebrew Peer-to-Peer Language Mentor Program
description: Peer-to-Peer Language Mentor Program in which student mentors work with student mentees to increase the language skills of both participants. One hour weekly meetings. HEBREW 10B is for language mentors.

title: Independent Study
description: Research paper required.

title: Truth, Lies, and History: The Uses of the Past
description: Focuses on how struggles over the meaning of historical events have shaped and continue to shape our understandings of the world. Through guest lectures from history faculty, students explore debates about the meaning of the past.

title: The Holocaust
description: Introduction to the history of European Jewish communities before the Holocaust; the origins of Nazi antisemitism; the implementation of the "Final Solution"; Jewish resistance to the Nazis; and attempts in film and literature to represent the Holocaust since 1945.

title: Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity Since WWII
description: Investigates instances of genocide since 1945 (including Cambodia, Bosnia, Rwanda, and others); explores the history behind these mass murders; considers why people kill, how victims survive, and questions whether international agreements are enough to prevent crimes against humanity.

title: Introductory Topics in History
description: Introduces methods and premises of historical study. Topics include introductions to cultural, political, economic, social, and religious history.

title: Native American History
description: Native American history in North America, emphasizing Indigenous perspectives and creative adaptations to changing historical circumstances. Topics may include sovereignty, settler colonialism, treaties, decolonization, Indigeneity; cultural, economic, religious, environmental practices and experiences; and intersections of U.S. and Native histories.

title: The U.S. and Asia
description: Explores the historical and contemporary transnational linkages between the U.S. and regions in Asia and their resultant flows of people, goods, and ideas. Attention given to the role of militarism and processes of globalization, and the histories of cultural contact/conflict.

title: Asian American Histories
description: Examines and compares diverse experiences of major Asian American groups since the mid-nineteenth century. Topics include origins of emigration; the formation and transformation of community; gender and family life; changing roles of Asian Americans in American society. Formerly ASIANAM 60A.

title: History of Sexuality in the US
description: Explores intertwined histories of sexuality, race, and gender in the US. By examining how different cultures and times viewed sexuality, race, and gender, it analyzes relationships between regulation of bodies/behaviors and construction of cultural, economic, and political norms.

title: What to Eat? Immigrants and the Development of American Cuisines
description: Relationship between immigration and changing American foodways; impact of several major culinary traditions of immigrants and racial minorities, such as African Americans, Asian Americans, Mexican Americans, Italian Americans, Irish Americans, and Jewish Americans, on America’s gastronomical and socioeconomic landscape.

title: Racial Segregation in Modern U.S
description: Explores the history of racial segregation in the United States from the late 19th century to the present.

title: World Religions I
description: An introduction to the history, doctrine, culture, and writing of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.

title: World Religions II
description: An introduction to various religious traditions in selected areas of the world—including India and South Asia, East Asia, Africa, and the Americas.

title: Religious Dialogue
description: Lectures and discussion on controversial topics in religion: sexual morality; religious violence; science; treatment of women and girls; religious truth, American Constitutional matters; secularization; the future of religion, and other topics.

title: Major Jewish Texts
description: Introduction to the diversity of Jewish cultures from ancient to modern times. Surveys the Jewish experience in various societies and civilizations: ancient Mediterranean, Middle East and North Africa, Europe, and the Americas.

title: World:  Innovations
description: Treats major themes of world historical development through the mid-17th century, focusing on the Eurasian world, but with secondary emphasis on Africa and the Americas.

title: World: Empires and Revolutions
description: Examines three major transformations that made the world of 1870 dramatically different from that of 1650: e.g., the scientific revolution, industrialization, and the formation of modern states and nations.

title: World: Nation, War, and Rights
description: Considers several major currents of modern history: technological change and its social effects; changes in gender relations; totalitarianism; peasant revolutions and the crisis of colonization; international migration; and ecological problems.

title: The Formation of Ancient Greek Society: Early Greece
description: A survey of ancient Greek civilization from its origins in the Bronze Age to the mid-Archaic period. Examines political and social history, as well as literature, art, religion, and archaeological remains.

title: The Formation of Ancient Greek Society:  Late Archaic and Classical Greece
description: A survey of ancient Greek civilization from the Late Archaic period to the Classical period. Focuses on major institutions and cultural phenomena as seen through the study of ancient Greek literature, history, archaeology, and religion.

title: The Formation of Ancient Greek Society: Fourth-Century and Hellenistic Greece
description: A survey of ancient Greek civilization from the fourth century BCE through to the Hellenistic period. Focuses on major institutions and cultural phenomena as seen through the study of ancient Greek literature, history, archaeology, and religion.

title: The Formation of Ancient Roman Society: Origins to Roman Republic
description: A survey of the development of Roman civilization from its eighth century BCE beginnings to the civil wars of the first century BCE. Examines political and social history, as well as literature, art, architecture, and religion.

title: The Formation of Ancient Roman Society: Roman Empire
description: A survey of Roman civilization from Augustus’s consolidation of power following the civil wars of the first century BCE to the crisis of the third century CE. Includes social history, literature, art, architecture, and religion.

title: The Formation of Ancient Roman Society: The Fall of Rome
description: A survey of Roman civilization from the crisis of the third century CE to the so-called “fall of Rome” in 476 CE.  Examines political and social history, as well as literature, art, architecture, and religion.

title: Colonial America:  New Worlds
description: Important themes in the social, economic, political, and cultural development in North America that transformed part of the geographical space into the U.S. Topics include Native Americans, European colonization, African enslavement, borderlands, gender, economic stratification, the American Revolution, the Constitution.

title: Nineteenth-Century U.S.: Crisis and Expansion
description: Explores the transformation of American society, economy, and politics during the 19th century. Topics include industrial revolution, slavery, antislavery, women's rights, reform movements, Civil War and Reconstruction, immigration and ethnicity, and cultural and social transformation.

title: Modern America:  Culture and Power
description: Important themes in U.S. history in the 20th and early 21st centuries. Topics include corporate capitalism, empire, immigration, race, gender, consumer society, World Wars, Progressiveness, New Deal, Great Society, civil rights, women's movements, Vietnam War, conservative politics, and economic stratification.

title: Crises and Revolutions
description: Study of turning points in world history, illustrating themes and methods of historical analysis.

title: The Making of Modern Science
description: Surveys the history of science and mathematics since the Scientific Revolution, examining central developments both chronologically and thematically, as well as investigating their significance for contemporary philosophical debates about the role and status of current scientific theories.

title: Problems in History: Asia
description: An introduction to the historical problems, the issues of interpretation, the primary sources, and the historical scholarship of the history of Asia, with an emphasis on developing skills in historical essay-writing.

title: Problems in History: Europe
description: An introduction to the historical problems, the issues of interpretation, the primary sources, and the historical scholarship of the history of Europe, with an emphasis on developing skills in historical essay-writing.

title: Problems in History: United States
description: An introduction to the historical problems, the issues of interpretation, the primary sources, and the historical scholarship of the history of the United States, with an emphasis on developing skills in historical essay-writing.

title: Problems in History: Latin America
description: An introduction to the historical problems, the issues of interpretation, the primary sources, and the historical scholarship of the history of Latin America, with an emphasis on developing skills in historical essay-writing.

title: Problems in History: Middle East and Africa
description: An introduction to the historical problems, the issues of interpretation, the primary sources, and the historical scholarship of the history of the Middle East and Africa, with an emphasis on developing skills in historical essay-writing.

title: Problems in History: Transregional History
description: An introduction to the historical problems, the issues of interpretation, the primary sources, and the historical scholarship of transregional history, with an emphasis on developing skills in historical essay-writing.

title: Writing About History
description: Specialized courses focusing on history writing and research skills. Each class reflects the instructor's intellectual interests and is conducted as a discussion group. Limited to 18 students. Several short writing assignments and one longer project meeting the upper-division writing requirement.

title: Topics in Environmental History
description: Explores the many historical interfaces between climate change, modes of production, and culture. Topics include the environmental history of warfare, imperialism, and famine in the nineteenth century and the history of environmental thought.

title: Topics in Medieval Europe
description: Theme-based approach to the main social, political, religious, and cultural developments in Europe between the 4th and 15th centuries. Topics include the Crusades, medieval towns, medieval visionaries, and the deadly sins.

title: Topics in Early Modern Europe
description: Theme-based approach to the main social, political, and cultural developments in Europe between the fifteenth and eighteenth centuries.  Topics included Renaissance humanism, Reformation and Counter-Reformation, scientific revolution, court culture and nation building, interactions with non-European peoples, cities and commerce.

title: Topics in Modern European History
description: Course content changes with instructor. Topics include the Inquisition; science and religion in modern Europe; sex and society in modern Europe; French revolutions; culture in interwar Europe; the Holocaust; the fall of communism in Eastern Europe.

title: War and Empire: France and the World in the 20th Century
description: Examines French experience of World War I, the defeat and occupation by Germany in World War II, the violent struggles that came with the loss of the French empire in the 1950s and 1960s, immigration, French responses to globalization.

title: Topics in Spanish History
description: Topics include Spain in the nineteenth century, the Spanish Civil War, and dictatorship and democracy in modern Spain.

title: The Era of World War II: 1933-45
description: The era of World War II and its political, social and economic history.

title: Topics in the History of Women in Europe
description: Studies in selected areas of history of European women. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Topics in the Jewish History
description: Studies in selected areas of Jewish history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: History of Antisemitism
description: Survey of the history of antisemitism, ancient to modern. Topics include medieval religious anti-Judaism, persecution of Jews and Judaism during the Crusades and the Inquisition, the rise of modern racial antisemitism, Nazi antisemitism, and the persistence of antisemitism since 1945.

title: Jews and Power
description: Examines the relationship between the Jewish people and political power over a 3500 year period. How have Jews preserved their communal interests and personal safety? How have they defined the proper relationship of the people to political authority?

title: History of Zoroastrianism
description: Reviews major trends in the history of Zoroastrianism.

title: Ancient Persia
description: Survey of the history of Persia in antiquity.

title: Medieval Persia
description: A survey of Persian history in the context of Late Antique and Medieval Islamic history.

title: Modern Iran
description: The history of Iran from the end of the Safavid Empire in the eighteenth century to the present.

title: The Emergence of the Modern Middle East
description: Offers a survey of the history of the Middle East from the 19th century to the present time. Formerly History 133A.

title: Women and Gender in the Middle East
description: Focuses on women and gender in the Middle East from the pre-modern to the contemporary.

title: Armenians and Armenia in Ancient to Early Modern World History
description: A history of Armenia and Armenians from ethnogenesis to the early modern period at the end of the 1700s within a regional and global context. Takes into account interactions and encounters with the empires, and peoples that encompassed their orbit.

title: Armenians and Armenia in Modern World History
description: Covers the most important themes in the history of Armenians and Armenia in the 19th, 20th, and 21st centuries within a regional (i.e., Middle East and Caucasus) and global context.

title: Topics in Middle Eastern History
description: Topics include the cultural, political, intellectual, social, and/or economic histories of one or many regions of the Middle East.

title: Africa: Societies and Cultures
description: Introduction to the variety of cultures, political organizations, social structures, and artistic expressions created by Africans over a broad time span. Indigenous development of African societies in distinct regions of the continent. Issues, themes, processes for understanding history of Africa.

title: Topics in the History of Africa
description: Studies in selected areas of African history. Topics addressed vary each quarter

title: Topics in South African History
description: Introduction to important historical events and processes in Southern Africa. Focuses on particular themes and explores how those themes change over time. Topics include: changing ideas about race, the development of class structures, identity formation, the role of gender.

title: History of the African Diaspora
description: Examines the causes and consequences of the multiple diasporas of African peoples since the sixteenth century in the Atlantic world, especially the Americas and Europe.

title: The Scientific Revolution
description: An examination of early modern European science from 1500-1700. Includes primary readings from central figures (Copernicus, Harvey, Bacon, Descartes, et al.); themes include the impact of printing, humanism, patronage, technology, and discussion of the term "revolution" in this context.

title: Topics in the History of Science and Technology
description: Studies in selected areas of science and technological history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Language Origins: Evolution, Genetics, and the Brain
description: Examines how human language(s) may have originated. Studies pertinent techniques (reconstruction) and addresses related questions, including is our language faculty inborn (i.e., genetically encoded)? Can brain imaging and population genetics research help to unlock this mystery of human evolution?

title: Topics in the History of Medicine and Health Care
description: Studies in selected areas of Medicine and Health Care history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Surviving Epidemics
description: Analyzes diverse social and cultural responses to epidemics from mid-nineteenth century to the present, including incarceration, caregiving, and disability rights. Using interdisciplinary materials and collaborative activities, students interpret strategies and outcomes of Indigenous, racialized, and marginalized communities surviving great illness.

title: Topics in U.S. History
description: Studies in selected areas of U.S. history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: California Dreaming: Conquest, Conflict, and Globalization in the Golden State
description: California as a case study of national trends and as a unique setting: its specific problems and culture. Major themes include: colonization, immigration, race relations, agricultural development, industrialization, urbanization, working class movements, social conflict, and political reform.

title: Topics in American Social and Economic History
description: Studies in selected areas of American social and economic history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Topics in American Cultural and Intellectual History
description: Studies in selected areas of American cultural and intellectual history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Topics in Women and Gender Relations in the United States
description: Studies in selected areas of women and gender relations in the United States. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Education and the American Dream: Historical Perspectives on Democracy and Education
description: Examines the relationship between public schooling and the promotion of democratic ideals in American society over the past two centuries.

title: Topics in Multicultural U.S. History
description: Examines the variety of cultural expressions through which the people who came to inhabit the United States historically signify their collective identities.

title: Topics in African American History
description: Studies in selected areas of African American history.  Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Chicana/Chicano History: Twentieth Century
description: Examines social history of the Southwest with emphasis on Mexican-origin people. Discusses major questions, theory and research methods pertinent to Chicana/Chicano history. Themes explored include: immigration, xenophobia, class struggle, leadership, generational cohorts, unionization, education, barrioization, ethnicity, patriarchy, sexuality.

title: Latinas in the Twentieth Century U.S
description: Latinas in the U.S. from 1900 to present, offering a diversity of their cultures, regional histories, sexualities, generations, and classes.

title: Latina/Latino Pop:  Latina/Latino Popular Culture
description: With a focus on the politics of language and space/place, prepares students to critically analyze sites of Latina/Latino popular culture including: music, film, performance, sports, media, and varied subcultures.

title: Topics in Asian-American History
description: Introduction to important themes in the history of people of Asian ancestry in the United States from the nineteenth century to the present.

title: Asian American Labor
description: Explores history of Asian Americans and work from the nineteenth century to the present. Areas of study include migration, colonialism, family, social organization, and work culture.

title: American Urban History
description: A study of urban communities in the United States, from colonial times to the present. Traces the impact of industrialization and urbanization on social and cultural life, and investigates the significance of urban life for U.S. democratic culture.

title: Sex and Conquest in Latin America
description: Competing ideas of masculinity and femininity, sexual violence, sexual identities, and gendered hierarchies informed how the Spanish engaged in military and religious domination of Mexican and Andean communities, as well as the forms of native resistance throughout colonial Latin America.

title: World of Coffee
description: History of consumption and production of coffee over the centuries, and coffee's cultural, economic, social, political consequences. Coffee's social life as a drug, symbol of hospitality, religious rite, sociability and bourgeois lifestyle, commodity, source of livelihoods, imperial revenues, corporate profits.

title: Caribbean History: Colonization to Emancipation
description: Exploration of the history of the archipelago from pre-Columbian times to the end of slavery; examining the impact of European colonization, decimation of the indigenous populations, African slavery, resistance, and emancipation; the unity and diversity of experience in region.

title: Caribbean History: Emancipation to Independence
description: Post-emancipation and anti-colonial struggles ending with political independence for most of the region. Examines social, political, economic, cultural dimensions of post-emancipation period, including large-scale migration to Central America, the U.S., and Britain; the region's global cultural and political contribution.

title: Revolution and Memory in Latin America
description: Comparison of how Andeans resurrected the leaders of the Inca, a defeated indigenous empire, to contest the Spanish empire with how enslaved Africans, with their descendants and others, defeated Spanish colonial rule in what would become the Cuban nation.

title: U.S. Intervention in Latin America
description: Explores political, economic, social, and cultural ties that bind Latin America to the United States. Focuses on U.S. intervention and Latin American response from early nineteenth century to present day. Case studies include Mexico, Guatemala, Cuba, Chile, and Central America.

title: Revolution and Reaction in Cold War Latin America
description: Explores Latin American experiences of revolutionary change and military dictatorship during the Cold War (1945-1990). Pays particular attention to the lives of women, peasants, workers, and the urban middle classes. Case studies include Guatemala, Cuba, Chile, Argentina, Nicaragua, and Mexico.

title: Cuban Society and Revolution
description: Explores the causes, development, and legacy of the 1959 Revolution. Themes include economic dependency, democracy, race, gender, culture, and the always volatile relations between Cuba and the United States.

title: Revolution in Latin America
description: Presents a comparative analysis of the causes, development, and consequences of selected revolutionary movements, focusing on outbreaks in Mexico, Bolivia, Cuba, Chile, Nicaragua, and Grenada.  Explores topics of state formation, economic nationalism, social justice, ethnicity, and role of international affairs.

title: Topics in Latin American History
description: Studies in selected areas of Latin American history.  Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Ancient India
description: Examines the visual and religious history of the region defined as "India" today, but necessarily encompassing modern Bangladesh and Pakistan.  Culminates with the supposed Golden Age of the Gupta empire and its far-reaching legacies.

title: Medieval India
description: Begins with the Gupta period's aesthetic legacies in South Asia's architecture, sculpture, and painting. Explores the dispersal of Islam throughout South Asia, including the Muslim communities of southern India.

title: Chinese History to 1800
description: A survey of the history of China to 1800.

title: Chinese History: 1800-1949
description: An examination of Chinese society and thought from the late-eighteenth century to the 1949 revolution. Focuses on the role of intellectuals; popular culture; women in Chinese society; developments in commerce and urban life; rebellion; foreign imperialism.

title: Topics in the History of China
description: Topics include the cultural, political, economic, social, intellectual, and religious history of China.

title: Topics in the History of Japan
description: Topics include the cultural, political, economic, social, intellectual and religious history of Japan.

title: Topics in the History of Korea
description: Topics include the cultural, political, economic, social, intellectual, and religious history of Korea.

title: Topics in the History of South Asia
description: Topics include the cultural, political, economic, social, intellectual, and religious history of South Asia.

title: Topics in the History of Southeast Asia
description: Topics include the cultural, political, economic, social, intellectual, and religious history of Southeast Asia.

title: The Viet Nam War Seen Through Oral History
description: Offers an overview of the Viet Nam War; explores the art of doing oral history and the use of oral testimony in research and writing the history of war through conducting oral history interviews with individuals of the war generation.

title: Special Studies in Social History
description: Studies in selected areas of social history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Special Studies in Intellectual-Cultural History
description: Studies in selected areas of intellectual-cultural history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Special Studies in International History
description: Studies in selected areas of international history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Special Studies in Comparative History
description: Studies in selected areas of comparative history. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Special Studies in Social Theory
description: Studies in selected areas of social theory. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Colloquium
description: Specialized courses dealing primarily with close reading and analysis of primary and secondary works; required reports and papers. Each colloquium reflects the instructor's intellectual interests and is conducted as a discussion group. Limited to 18 students.

title: Advanced Research Seminar I
description: The first course in a two-quarter advanced research sequence, this course allows upper-division history majors to undertake significant research and writing under close faculty supervision.

title: Advanced Research Seminar II
description: Second course in a two-quarter advanced research sequence. Allows upper division history majors to undertake significant research and writing under close faculty supervision.

title: Internships in Public History
description: Students will sharpen their abilities to research, critically interpret, and present history by “doing history” beyond the formal classroom. Internships, which introduce students to the field of “public history,” include working as archivists, oral historians, project advisers, and exhibit curators.

title: Independent Reading
description: Investigation of special topics through directed reading. Paper required.

title: History and Theory
description: Explores a variety of theoretical issues and methodological concerns that have sparked lively debate in the humanities and social sciences in past decades and which remain of urgent concern to 21st-century historical writing.

title: First-Year Research Seminar
description: Introduction to historical methodologies and preparation for the first-year research paper. Required of all first-year doctoral students and M.A. students.

title: First-Year Research Seminar
description: Research and writing of a paper demonstrating command of historical methods explored in HISTORY 202A . Required of all first-year Ph.D. students and M.A. students.

title: Second-Year Research Seminar
description: Part one of a two-quarter sequence required of all Ph.D. students during the second year of the program; not required for M.A. students. Includes primary research and writing a research paper, often related to a future dissertation topic.

title: Second-Year Research Seminar
description: Part two of a two-quarter sequence required of all Ph.D. students. Taken during the second year of the Ph.D. program; not required for M.A. students. Includes primary research and writing a research paper, often related to a future dissertation topic.

title: Approaches to History
description: Introduces graduate students to some of the most foundational ideas and debates that have shaped historiographical practice over the past half-century.

title: History in the Professions: An Introduction to Doing History in Graduate School and Beyond
description: Centers conversations and topics that illuminate the hidden curriculum of graduate school, explore the political economy of labor in the university, and provide students a foundational introduction to the historical profession.

title: History in the Professions: An Introduction to Doing History in Graduate School and Beyond
description: Centers conversations and topics that illuminate the hidden curriculum of graduate school, explore the political economy of labor in the university, and provide students a foundational introduction to the historical profession.

title: History in the Professions: An Introduction to Doing History in Graduate School and Beyond
description: Centers conversations and topics that illuminate the hidden curriculum of graduate school, explore the political economy of labor in the university, and provide students a foundational introduction to the historical profession.

title: Topics in European History
description: Studies in selected themes and time periods in European history. Topics and period vary.

title: Topics in World History
description: Studies in selected themes and time periods in world history. Topics and period vary.

title: Topics in Latin American History
description: Studies in selected themes and time periods in Latin American history. Topics and period vary.

title: Topics in American History
description: Studies in selected themes and time periods in U.S. history. Topics and period vary.

title: Topics in Middle Eastern History
description: Studies in selected themes and time periods in Middle Eastern history. Topics and period vary.

title: Topics in Asian History
description: Studies in selected themes and time periods in Asian history. Topics and period vary.

title: Special Topics
description: Lectures, readings, and discussion on subjects more limited in scope than those included in the year-long colloquium series.

title: Directed Reading
description: Reading courses focused on specialized topics.

title: History Internship
description: Limited to History graduate students with Department-approved internships.

title: Experimental Group Study
description: Open to four or more students.

title: Dissertation Research
description: Specifically designed for students researching and writing their dissertations.

title: University Teaching
description: Limited to Teaching Assistants.

title: Humanities Core Lecture
description: Through a range of cultural traditions and an interdisciplinary approach to works of literature, history, philosophy, and contemporary media, students are introduced to the humanities.

title: Humanities Core Lecture
description: Through a range of cultural traditions and an interdisciplinary approach to works of literature, history, philosophy, and contemporary media, students are introduced to the humanities.

title: Humanities Core Lecture
description: Through a range of cultural traditions and an interdisciplinary approach to works of literature, history, philosophy, and contemporary media, students are introduced to the humanities.

title: Masterpieces of Literature
description: Students closely read major works of world literature that are significant (1) in their own right, (2) for a specific literary tradition, and (3) because of their reception in other cultural contexts. Literature written in English and English translation.

title: Foundations of Bilingual Education and Bilingualism
description: Provides a comprehensive overview of current issues in bilingual education and bilingualism. Topics include dimensions of bilingualism, the effects of bilingualism on children's linguistic and cognitive development, bilingual education programs, literacy, special needs, and assessment.

title: Professional Development
description: Introduces Humanities undergraduates to various field study and experiential learning opportunities in Humanities and at UCI. Includes internship and research exploration and preparation activities designed to equip students with the skills necessary for career readiness and professional development.

title: Personal and Social Resilience in the Age of the Climate Crisis
description: In our rapidly changing world, many climate and other interrelated global crises are undermining human well-being. Students gain invaluable personal and social resilience skills (e.g., mindfulness), and implement collaborative high impact projects to navigate and build a meaningful future.

title: Humanities Out There (H.O.T.) Practicum
description: H.O.T. sponsors tutoring opportunities in local schools and after-school sites for UCI students. Requirements include weekly one-hour training sessions at UCI; at least eight off-site tutoring sessions; and short writing assignments.

title: Topics in Humanities First-Generation Seminar
description: Special topics related to the support of first-generation undergraduates pursing a Humanities degree.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Directed group study on special topics.

title: Directed Research
description: Directed research for senior Humanities students.

title: Critical Theory Workshop
description: A year-long Critical Theory Workshop, conducted by a team of instructors, conceived as a reading group, and developed with the input of all participants, where significant texts are discussed and analyzed in class.

title: Critical Theory Workshop
description: A year-long Critical Theory Workshop, conducted by a team of instructors, conceived as a reading group, and developed with the input of all participants, where significant texts are discussed and analyzed in class.

title: Critical Theory Workshop
description: A year-long Critical Theory Workshop, conducted by a team of instructors, conceived as a reading group, and developed with the input of all participants, where significant texts are discussed and analyzed in class.

title: Race and Justice Studies Writing Seminar
description: A required writing-intensive seminar conducted by an instructor affiliated with the Race and Justice Studies emphasis. Students with manuscripts on relevant topics will read and critique peer manuscripts, and revise manuscripts toward completion of articles, dissertation chapters, and other publications.

title: Latin American Studies
description: Students discuss significant texts on Latin America from a range of disciplines in the humanities, social sciences, and the arts.

title: Issues In Latin American Studies I
description: Students discuss significant texts on Latin America from a range of disciplines in the humanities, social sciences, and the arts. This course will meet 3-4 times per quarter throughout the academic year.

title: Issues In Latin American Studies II
description: Students discuss significant texts on Latin America from a range of disciplines in the humanities, social sciences, and the arts. This course will meet 3-4 times per quarter throughout the academic year.

title: Issues In Latin American Studies III
description: Students discuss significant texts on Latin America from a range of disciplines in the humanities, social sciences, and the arts. This course will meet 3-4 times per quarter throughout the academic year.

title: Advanced Critical Theory
description: Seminars on various topics in critical theory.

title: Humanities Out There (H.O.T.) Practicum
description: Individual study with H.O.T. faculty supervisor.

title: Foreign Language Teaching: Approaches and Methods
description: Introduces approaches and methods of foreign language learning and teaching, and the theoretical models of second-language acquisition and teaching. Focus areas include lesson planning, teaching vocabulary, grammar, speaking, reading, writing, listening, culture, task-based teaching, uses of digital media.

title: Foreign Language Teaching: Approaches and Methods
description: Introduces approaches and methods of foreign language learning and teaching, and the theoretical models of second-language acquisition and teaching. Focus areas include lesson planning, teaching vocabulary, grammar, speaking, reading, writing, listening, culture, task-based teaching, uses of digital media.

title: University Teaching
description: Limited to Teaching Associates in Humanities Core course.

title: Introduction to Software Engineering
description: Concepts, methods, and current practice of software engineering. Large-scale software production, software life cycle models, principles and techniques for each stage of development.

title: Special Topics in Informatics
description: Designed for informatics instructors to pilot new teaching materials and prototype new class. The format of the class is flexible based on the content to be offered in the future.

title: Concepts in Programming Languages
description: In-depth study of several contemporary programming languages stressing variety in data structures, operations, notation, and control. Examination of different programming paradigms, such as logic programming, functional programming and object-oriented programming; implementation strategies, programming environments, and programming style.

title: Requirements Analysis and Engineering
description: Equips students to develop techniques of software-intensive systems through successful requirements analysis techniques and requirements engineering. Students learn systematic process of developing requirements through cooperative problem analysis, representation, and validation.

title: Software Testing, Analysis, and Quality Assurance
description: Preparation for developing high-quality software through successful veriﬁcation and validation techniques. Fundamental principles of software testing, implementing software testing practices, ensuring the thoroughness of testing to gain conﬁdence in the correctness of the software.

title: Project in Software System Design
description: Specification, design, construction, testing, and documentation of a complete software system. Special emphasis on the need for and use of teamwork, careful planning, and other techniques for working with large systems.

title: Engineering Artificial Intelligence Software
description: Design, deploy, and manage software incorporating AI components in real-world production environments. Topics include types of models, in-house vs. third-party models, retrieval augmented generation, fine-tuning, prompt engineering, model testing, and reliability.

title: Software Design: Applications
description: Introduction to application design: designing the overall functionality of a software application. Topics include general design theory, software design theory, and software architecture. Includes practice in designing and case studies of existing designs.

title: Software Design: Structure and Implementation
description: Introduction to implementation design: designing the internals of a software application. Topics include design aesthetics, design implementation, design recovery, design patterns, and component reuse. Includes practice in designing and case studies of existing designs.

title: Internet Applications Engineering
description: Concepts in Internet applications engineering with emphasis on the Web. Peer-to-Peer and Interoperability. Topics include HTTP and REST, Remote Procedure/Method Calls, Web Services, data representations, content distribution networks, identity management, relevant W3C/IETF standards, and relevant new large-scale computing styles.

title: Computer Game Development
description: Introduction to the principles of interactive 2D and 3D computer game development. Concepts in computer graphics, algorithms, software engineering, art and graphics, music and sound, story analysis, and artificial intelligence are presented and are the basis for student work.

title: Human Computer Interaction
description: Basic principles of human-computer interaction (HCI). Introduces students to user interface design techniques, design guidelines, and usability testing. Students gain the ability to design and evaluate user interfaces and become familiar with some of the outstanding research problems in HCI.

title: Advanced Topics in Human-Computer Interaction
description: Advanced material in the space of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) through application and practice. Introduces user research methods such as observation, interviewing, usability testing, design activities, and user analytics.

title: User Interaction Software
description: Introduction to human-computer interaction programming. Emphasis on current tools, standards, methodologies for implementing effective interaction designs. Widget toolkits, Web interface programming, geo-spatial and map interfaces, mobile phone interfaces.

title: Advanced Topics in User Interface Software
description: Advanced material in the space of human-computer interaction programming. Introduces core interface toolkit concepts including interface states, event handling, layout and positioning, and custom interactions.  Materials Fee

title: Information Retrieval
description: An introduction to information retrieval including indexing, retrieval, classifying, and clustering text and multimedia documents.

title: Information Visualization
description: Introduction to interactive visual interfaces for large datasets, and to principles of human visual perception and human computer interaction that inform their design. Various applications for data analysis and monitoring are discussed.

title: Designing for the Internet of Things
description: Introduction to design principles for Internet of Things applications. Prototyping, design, and evaluation of physical computing applications, smart environments, embedded systems, and future computing scenarios. Includes hands-on in-class laboratory exercises. Materials Fee

title: Project Management
description: Introduces theoretical and practical aspects of project management. Topics include organizational theory, group behavior, project management skills, case studies, personal and group productivity tools, management of distributed work, stakeholders, consultants, and knowledge management. Students do a project exercise.

title: Design of Social and Interpersonal Technologies
description: Considerations around the design of technologies beyond the individual. Topics may include social media, messaging and communication, feeds, algorithmic recommendations, privacy, and access.

title: Social Analysis of Computing
description: Examines current computing technologies and their consequences at the individual, group, community, and societal level. Topics may include privacy, trust, identity, reputation, governance, ethics, social justice, and culture.

title: Information Systems in Organizations
description: Introduction to role of information systems in organizations, components and structure of organizational information systems, and techniques used in information systems analysis, design, and implementation.

title: Project in Organization Analysis of Information Systems
description: Students undertake a project in analyzing the use of information systems in organizations. Introduces research methods such as observation, interviewing, and document analysis. Students gain experience with techniques including jotting, writing field notes, qualitative coding, and memoing.

title: Children's Learning and Media
description: Examines how popular media may impact how young people learn, develop, and communicate by looking at research related to the impacts of a wide range of popular media including television, video games, digital environments, mobile devices, and other multimedia.

title: Introduction to Health Informatics
description: Broad overview of medical informatics for students with varied backgrounds. Electronic medical records, online resources, mobile technologies, patient safety, and computational design. Legal, ethical, and public policy issues. Health systems management. Evaluation and fieldwork for health systems.

title: Project in Health Informatics
description: Students undertake significant quarter-long projects related to health informatics. Topics may include field evaluations of health care technologies, prototypes, iterative design, and system implementations.

title: Consumer Health Informatics
description: Consumer health informatics is a subfield of health informatics aimed to empower healthcare consumers through a variety of consumer-facing technologies. Covers health behavior change, health literacy, social support, and the design, evaluation, and implementation of major technological applications.

title: Health Data Analytics
description: The increasing availability of electronic heath data provides an unprecedented opportunity to use data-driven approaches to improve human health. Equips students with essential skills in curating, managing, and analyzing health data, focusing on electronic health records.

title: Special Topics in Informatics
description: Studies in selected areas of informatics. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Senior Design Project
description: Group supervised project in which students analyze, specify, design, construct, evaluate, and adapt a significant information processing system. Topics include team management, professional ethics, and systems analysis.

title: Senior Design Project
description: Group supervised project in which students analyze, specify, design, construct, evaluate, and adapt a significant information processing system. Topics include team management, professional ethics, and systems analysis.

title: Individual Study
description: Individual research or investigation under the direction of an individual faculty member.

title: Research Methodology for Informatics
description: Introduction to strategies and idioms of research in Informatics. Includes examination of issues in scientific inquiry, qualitative and quantitative methods, and research design. Both classic texts and contemporary research literature are read and analyzed.

title: Qualitative Research Methods in Information Systems
description: Introduction to qualitative research methods used to study computerization and information systems, such as open-ended interviewing, participant observation, and ethnography. Studies of the methods in practice through examination of research literature.

title: Quantitative Research Methods in Information Systems
description: Quantitative research methods used to study computerization and information systems. Design of instruments, sampling, sample sizes, and data analysis. Validity and reliability. Longitudinal versus cross-sectional designs. Analysis of secondary data. Studies of the methods through examination of research literature.

title: Doctoral Seminar on Research and Writing
description: Doctoral seminar centered on original research and writing. Provides a chance for doctoral students at all levels to present original work, brainstorm ongoing issues, and learn to provide and receive critical feedback from peers.

title: Seminar in Informatics
description: Current research and research trends in informatics. Forum for presentation and criticism by students of research work in progress.

title: User Interface Design and Evaluation
description: Introduction to the design and evaluation of user interfaces, with an emphasis on methodology. Cognitive principles, design life cycle, on-line and off-line prototyping techniques. Toolkits and architectures for interactive systems. Evaluation techniques, including heuristic and laboratory methods.

title: Research in Human-Centered Computing
description: Introduction to contemporary topics in human-computer interaction, including methods, technologies, design, and evaluation. Emerging application domains and their challenges to traditional research methods. Advanced architectures and technologies. Critical issues.

title: Introduction to Ubiquitous Computing
description: The "disappearing computer" paradigm. Differences to the desktop computing model: applications, interaction in augmented environments, security, alternate media, small operating systems, sensors, and embedded systems design. Evaluation by project work and class participation.

title: Introduction to Embedded and Ubiquitous Systems
description: Embedded and ubiquitous system technologies including processors, DSP, memory, and software. System interfacing basics; communication strategies; sensors and actuators, mobile and wireless technology. Using pre-designed hardware and software components. Design case studies in wireless, multimedia, and/or networking domains.

title: Computer-Supported Cooperative Work
description: The role of information systems in supporting work in groups and organizations. Examines various technologies designed to support communication, information sharing, and coordination. Focuses on behavioral and social aspects of designing and using group support technologies.

title: Social Analysis of Computing
description: The social and economic impacts of computing and information technologies on groups, organizations, and society. Topics include computerization and changes in the character of work, social control and privacy, electronic communities, and risks of safety-critical systems to people.

title: Computerization, Work, and Organizations
description: Selected topics in the influence of computerization and information systems in transforming work and organizations. Theories of organization and organizational change. Processes by which diverse information technologies influence changes in work and organizations over short and long time periods.

title: Theories of Information Society
description: Social and economic conceptions of information technology. Macrosocial and economic conditions that foster changes in information technologies. Social construction of information and computer technology in professional worlds. Theories of information technology and large-scale social change.

title: Digital Media and Society
description: Selected topics in the technological and social aspects of online interactions, and policy including online games, social media, electronic activism, e-commerce, and digital libraries. Media-theoretic approaches to digital technology. Architectures, infrastructure considerations, and their consequences.

title: Information Technology in Global Sustainability
description: Explores the relationship between recent developments in information technology and the global transition to sustainability. Topics include the role of IT systems in the provision of human needs and wants (e.g., smart grids, food systems, and other IT-enabled infrastructure).

title: Overview of Human-Computer Interaction and Design
description: Introduction to human-computer interaction and user-centered design. The material is focused on laying the groundwork for understanding the history, importance, and methods of human-computer interaction and design.

title: User Needs Analysis
description: Understanding the user's context, needs, and preferences. Topics include interviews and observations, modeling the context, flow, culture, space, and artifacts involved in an endeavor, ways of aggregating what is found, and presenting these findings to others.

title: Design and Prototyping
description: Introduction to user-centered design and prototyping. Focused on practical methods for interaction design. Topics include the nature of design and the challenges to creating and evaluating good designs, as well specific skills for designing interactive systems.

title: User Experience Evaluation
description: Evaluating prototypes and completed systems. Topics include comparative analysis, laboratory experiments, heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthroughs, surveys, clickstreams, and help-desk.

title: Advanced Design and Prototyping
description: Develop and communicate interactive technology design prototypes. Moving concepts from brainstorming and paper prototypes to wireframe and limited functionality mock-ups.

title: Interactive Technology Studio
description: Technologies, languages, and skills required for creating prototypes to communicate interactive technology concepts. Topics include HTTP, CSS, CSS scripting, AJAX, Design Patterns, Javascript, Javascript libraries such as jQuery, SQL, MVC, and cloud architectures.

title: Innovations in HCI and Design
description: Recent social and technological developments in human-computer interaction and design. Topics will vary as the field progresses but include novel input techniques, novel platforms, and innovations in theory and methods of design.

title: Capstone Project in HCI and Design
description: Group project that reinforces all concepts learned in this program, including knowing where user experience work is most appropriate and essential, and executing the appropriate steps.

title: Capstone Project and Portfolio
description: Completion of capstone projects and development of portfolios. Ideation, critique, development, and critique.

title: Special Topics in Applying Human-Computer Interaction and Design in Industry
description: Selected topics related to understanding and applying human-computer interaction and design concepts and practices within the User Experience industry. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Research Seminar
description: Forum for presentation and criticism by students of research work in progress. Presentation of problem areas and related work. Specific goals and progress of research.

title: Special Topics in Informatics
description: Studies in selected areas of informatics. Topics addressed vary each quarter.

title: Thesis Supervision
description: Individual research or investigation conducted in preparation for the M.S. thesis option or the dissertation requirements for the Ph.D. program.

title: Individual Study
description: Individual research or investigation under the direction of an individual faculty member.

title: Computing Technologies and Their Social Impact
description: Examines current computing technologies and social implications at the individual, group, and societal level. Issues include privacy, trust, identity, reputation, governance, and malicious behavior, ethics, social justice, and culture and community.

title: Designing Computing Technology for People
description: Introduces students to basic principles of human-centered computing and theories of human behavior that are relevant for the design of technology for people. Specific topics include usability, prototyping, heuristic evaluation, visual design, theory of perception, accessibility.

title: Global Disruption and Information Technology
description: Explores how new forms of information technology may support transition to a sustainable civilization. Topics include design and implementation of IT systems, science of global change, online community building, and “green IT”. Activities involve reading, writing, discussion, and final project.

title: Boolean Logic and Discrete Structures
description: Relations and their properties; Boolean algebras, formal languages; finite automata.

title: Discrete Mathematics for Computer Science
description: Covers essential tools from discrete mathematics used in computer science with an emphasis on the process of abstracting computational problems and analyzing them mathematically. Topics include mathematical induction, combinatorics, and recurrence relations.

title: Computational Linear Algebra
description: Matrices and linear transformations, systems of linear equations, determinants, linear vector spaces, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, orthogonal matrices, diagonalization, and least squares. Topics taught primarily from an algorithmic perspective, including computational solutions, applications, and numerical error analysis.

title: Introduction to Computation for Scientists and Engineers
description: Introduces principles, techniques, and computational tools for quantitative approach to basic problem solving in physics and engineering. Project-based course that actively explores how programming techniques are used for solving STEM real-world problems.

title: How Computers Work
description: Introduction to digital computer and communication systems. Capabilities and limitations of information technology. Representing information in digital form. Overview of computer organization, internet, operating systems, software. Human-computer interaction and social impact.

title: The Internet and Public Policy
